document.title: Brick Spider
document.description: A web app that encrypts secret messages into downloadable PNG images of procedural brick-style abstract artwork, then decrypts those images back into messages using a passphrase.

body.raw:
## Overview

Brick Spider is a web app that encrypts text messages into downloadable PNG images of procedural brick-style abstract artwork, then decrypts those images back into messages using a passphrase. Type a message, enter a passphrase, and the app generates a stylized brick sculpture that secretly contains your encrypted text. Another user can upload that image, enter the correct passphrase, and recover the original message.

The concept is inspired by the 2001 movie *Along Came a Spider*, in which students use cryptographic messages hidden in images to pass notes in class. Brick Spider takes that idea and wraps it in a late-90s hacker aesthetic — chunky beveled panels, terminal-style headings, CRT scanlines, and a palette of neon greens, teals, ambers, and magentas on near-black backgrounds. The interface feels like a playful toy hacking utility you'd find on a school computer lab machine in 1998.

The entire encode and decode pipeline runs client-side in the browser. No messages, passphrases, or uploaded images ever leave the device.

## The Problem It Solves

Sending a secret message digitally usually means trusting a platform with your plaintext — an email provider, a messaging app, a cloud service. Even end-to-end encrypted messengers require both parties to be on the same platform. Brick Spider removes that dependency entirely. The output is a PNG image — a file format that works everywhere, can be shared through any channel, and doesn't raise suspicion. It looks like abstract art. It contains an encrypted message.

The app also solves the problem of making steganography approachable. Traditional steganography tools are command-line utilities with opaque interfaces and no visual feedback. Brick Spider makes the process tangible: you watch the brick sculpture render in real-time, you can preview it before downloading, and the decode flow is as simple as dragging a PNG onto a dropzone.

## Encryption

Brick Spider uses AES-256-GCM via the Web Crypto API with PBKDF2-SHA-256 key derivation. When a user enters a message and passphrase:

1. A random 16-byte salt and 12-byte IV are generated.
2. The passphrase is expanded into a 256-bit key using PBKDF2 with 310,000 iterations.
3. The message is encrypted with AES-256-GCM, producing ciphertext with an authenticated tag.
4. A binary payload is assembled with magic bytes (`BSPDR`), version, crypto scheme, style preset, salt, nonce, ciphertext, and a CRC32 checksum.

The payload is then converted to a 2-bit-per-symbol bitstream and mapped onto a grid of brick cells.

```typescript
const MAGIC_BYTES = "BSPDR";
const FORMAT_VERSION = 1;
const CRYPTO_SCHEME_AES_GCM_PBKDF2 = 1;
const PBKDF2_ITERATIONS = 310_000;
```

All encryption and decryption happens in the browser using the Web Crypto API. No plaintext, no passphrases, no keys, and no ciphertext are ever sent to a server.

## Image Encoding

The encrypted binary payload is encoded into a fixed 64×64 logical grid rendered on a 1024×1024 canvas. Each cell encodes 2 bits of data using one of four exact RGB colors:

| Bits | Color | Hex |
|------|-------|-----|
| 00 | Dark teal | `#00ff66` |
| 01 | Neon green | `#00ccff` |
| 10 | Amber | `#ffcc00` |
| 11 | Magenta | `#ff33cc` |

A 64×64 grid provides 4,096 cells × 2 bits = 8,192 bits = 1,024 bytes of raw capacity — enough for messages up to 500 characters after encryption overhead.

Four registration markers in the grid corners confirm the image is a valid Brick Spider artifact before decoding begins. The decoder samples the center 4×4 pixels of each cell to read the exact data color, with a tolerance of ±8 per RGB channel to handle minor rendering drift.

```typescript
const DATA_COLORS = {
  0: "#00ff66",
  1: "#00ccff",
  2: "#ffcc00",
  3: "#ff33cc",
};
```

## Procedural Brick Artwork

The image generator renders toy-brick-like visual objects using deterministic procedural rules — no AI, no external image services. Each brick cell is drawn as a stylized top-down brick with a rectangular body, rounded corners, raised studs, subtle highlights, and subtle shadows.

The key constraint is that the center sample square of each data cell must be the exact palette color, while decorative shading can appear outside that region. This keeps the image decodable while still looking like abstract art rather than a bare grid code.

Three visual presets are available:

### Spider Totem

An abstract vertical sculpture with a central body cluster and leg-like radial brick extensions. Slightly eerie but playful — the intended signature look of the app.

### Cyber Bloom

A radial flower-like brick arrangement with bright hacker palette colors, dense geometric symmetry, and neon accents. More vibrant and kaleidoscopic than the other presets.

### Classroom Relic

A chunky asymmetric object that looks like something built under a school desk. Muted computer-lab palette with subdued greens and ambers. The most understated of the three.

Unused grid cells are filled with decorative deterministic noise based on the payload hash, ensuring every generated image is unique even with the same style preset.

## Decode Flow

Decoding reverses the entire pipeline. The user uploads a PNG and enters a passphrase:

1. The image is loaded onto a hidden canvas at its original dimensions.
2. Registration markers are located in the grid corners.
3. The center of each cell is sampled and mapped back to a 2-bit symbol.
4. The bitstream is reassembled into bytes.
5. Magic bytes and CRC32 checksum are validated.
6. The salt and IV are extracted from the payload header.
7. The passphrase is expanded into a key using PBKDF2 with the extracted salt.
8. AES-256-GCM decrypts the ciphertext.
9. The plaintext message is displayed.

A wrong passphrase produces a clear error — AES-GCM's authentication tag prevents partial or garbled plaintext from leaking. Invalid or modified images are rejected at the marker detection and checksum validation stages.

## Technology

### Web Crypto API

All cryptographic operations use the browser-native Web Crypto API. `SubtleCrypto.deriveKey()` handles PBKDF2 key derivation, while `SubtleCrypto.encrypt()` and `SubtleCrypto.decrypt()` handle AES-256-GCM. No external crypto libraries are required.

### Canvas API

The HTML Canvas 2D API handles both rendering and decoding. The renderer draws the brick grid with decorative elements (studs, highlights, shadows) while preserving exact center-sample colors. The decoder reads pixel data from a hidden canvas using `getImageData()`.

### Next.js and TypeScript

The app is built with Next.js App Router and TypeScript. Routes follow a simple structure: a landing page, an encode page, a decode page, and an about page. All state management uses React local state — no external stores, no persistence, no server-side storage.

### Tailwind CSS

The UI is styled with Tailwind CSS extended with a late-90s hacker palette. Near-black and deep navy backgrounds, CRT-green and amber accents, chunky beveled borders, pixel-font terminal headings, and optional scanline overlays. The interface is designed to feel like desktop software from 1998 — playful, slightly janky, and unmistakably a hacking utility.

## UI Design

The interface is deliberately retro. Fake window chrome wraps each section. Buttons have chunky beveled edges that compress on click. Status indicators use green and amber lights. Dividers are glitchy and asymmetric. Terminal-style monospace headings sit alongside pixel-font accents.

The encode page features a message textarea with a live character counter (max 500), passphrase inputs with show/hide toggles, a style preset selector, and a preview panel that renders the generated brick artifact in real-time. The decode page has a drag-and-drop upload zone, an image preview, a passphrase input, and a result panel.

Mobile responsiveness is a first-class concern. The layout collapses to a single column on small screens, all inputs go full-width, and tap targets are sized at a minimum of 44×44 pixels. The preview image scales to screen width. Hover-only interactions are avoided entirely.

## What I Learned

- The Web Crypto API is synchronous in its interface but asynchronous in execution. Every operation returns a Promise, which chains nicely with React's async state updates but requires careful error handling at each step.
- Encoding binary data into an image grid sounds straightforward until you need to handle variable payload lengths, registration markers, and decorative fill — all while keeping the center sample pixels exact. The margin for error is zero: a single shifted pixel during rendering can corrupt the entire decode.
- AES-GCM authentication tags make wrong-passphrase errors clean and unambiguous. There's no need for a separate integrity check beyond the CRC32, but having both provides defense in depth — the checksum catches image corruption before attempting decryption, while the auth tag catches key mismatches.
- Canvas `getImageData()` returns raw RGBA values, and the exact pixel values depend on the canvas rendering pipeline. Ensuring that drawn colors survive a render-read roundtrip required avoiding any compositing, anti-aliasing, or alpha blending in the data cell regions.
- Building a retro UI that feels authentic without being unreadable is a balancing act. Chunky borders and pixel fonts add character, but they need to stay functional. The solution was to keep form elements clean and accessible while pushing the decorative treatment into chrome, panels, and headings.

## Challenges

- **Pixel-perfect encoding**: The most critical challenge was ensuring that colors drawn to the canvas are exactly the colors read back during decode. Canvas 2D rendering can introduce subtle color shifts from anti-aliasing, compositing modes, or sub-pixel positioning. The solution was to draw data cells as filled rectangles at integer coordinates with `imageSmoothingEnabled` disabled, and to sample from a guaranteed-exact center region.
- **Payload capacity**: Fitting an encrypted 500-character message into a 64×64 grid required careful payload design. The binary header (magic bytes, version, scheme, lengths) and CRC32 checksum consume bytes before the ciphertext begins. Keeping the header compact while remaining self-describing took several iterations.
- **Mobile canvas performance**: Generating a 1024×1024 brick grid with per-cell decorative rendering involves thousands of draw calls. On mobile devices, this can take long enough to freeze the UI. The solution was to use `requestAnimationFrame` batching and show a progress indicator during generation.
- **Retro aesthetic without irony**: The late-90s hacker look can easily tip into parody. The goal was to make it feel like a real utility that happens to have personality, not a joke app. This meant keeping the crypto implementation serious, the error messages helpful, and the workflow efficient — then layering the visual theme on top.

## Privacy

No message text, passphrases, derived keys, or uploaded images are ever sent to a server. The entire encode and decode pipeline runs in the browser using the Web Crypto API and Canvas API. The app has no analytics, no tracking pixels, and no third-party scripts that could exfiltrate data. Generated PNG images contain no metadata — no EXIF, no text chunks, no identifying information beyond the encoded pixel data.

## Limitations

Decoding only works for unmodified Brick Spider PNGs. JPEG conversion, screenshots, cropped or resized images, social media compression, and printed-and-photographed images will not decode successfully. This is a deliberate tradeoff — optimizing for lossy decode would require error-correcting codes and fuzzy color matching that significantly increase complexity and reduce capacity.

## Links

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/ppsandwich/brick-spider)
- [Live App](https://brick-spider.vercel.app)

document.title: Expecto
document.description: An interactive neural network visualizer built with TensorFlow.js and Canvas that lets you build, train, and poke neural networks in real-time.

body.raw:
## Overview

Expecto is an interactive neural network visualizer that makes the inner workings of a neural network tangible and playful. You build a network by adding and removing layers and neurons, choose an activation function, pick a dataset, and hit Train. As the network learns, you watch connections thicken and color based on weight values, signal particles flow between neurons, and decision boundaries paint themselves across the canvas.

The core idea is that neural network toolkits are either too abstract (loss curves and tensor shapes) or too academic (static diagrams in textbooks). Expecto occupies the middle ground: a live, interactive sandbox where you can drag neurons around, click inputs to toggle them, and see exactly what each neuron is doing at any given moment. No install, no setup, no server — just open the page and start poking.

![Expecto full interface with network visualization, control panel, and stats bar](/images/projects/expecto-full.png)

## The Problem It Solves

Most neural network educational tools fall into two categories. The first is the "tutorial notebook" — a Jupyter or Colab notebook that trains a model and plots a loss curve. These are informative but passive. You run the cell, you see the output, you don't interact. The second is the "textbook diagram" — a static illustration of circles and arrows with labels like "hidden layer 1." These give you the vocabulary but not the intuition.

What's missing is the middle layer: a tool where you can *feel* the weights changing, where backpropagation looks like something physical happening, where you can break a network and see *why* it breaks. Expecto fills that gap. It's a sandbox, not a tutorial. There are no right answers, no progress bars, no curriculum. You poke it and it squirms.

## Development Process

The app is built entirely in vanilla JavaScript with no build tools, no bundler, and no framework. TensorFlow.js handles the machine learning, and the HTML Canvas 2D API handles all rendering. The whole thing is three files: `index.html`, `style.css`, and `script.js`.

### Neural Network Engine

The `NeuralNetwork` class wraps a TensorFlow.js sequential model. The architecture is stored as a plain array (default `[2, 4, 4, 1]` — two inputs, two hidden layers of four neurons each, one output), and the model is rebuilt from scratch whenever the architecture changes.

```javascript
buildModel() {
  const layers = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < this.architecture.length - 1; i++) {
    const units = this.architecture[i + 1];
    const isLast = i === this.architecture.length - 2;
    layers.push(
      tf.layers.dense({
        inputShape: i === 0 ? [this.architecture[0]] : undefined,
        units,
        activation: isLast ? "sigmoid" : this.activation,
        kernelInitializer: "glorotUniform",
        biasInitializer: "zeros",
      })
    );
  }
  this.model = tf.sequential({ layers });
  this.model.compile({
    optimizer: tf.train.adam(0.01),
    loss: "binaryCrossentropy",
  });
}
```

Three activation functions are supported: tanh (default), ReLU, and sigmoid. The output layer always uses sigmoid since all three datasets are binary classification problems. The optimizer is Adam with a user-adjustable learning rate (logarithmic scale from 0.0001 to 0.1, default 0.01).

The architecture is mutable at runtime. Users can add or remove hidden layers and add or remove neurons from all hidden layers simultaneously. Each mutation disposes the old model, rebuilds, and re-extracts weights for visualization. The parameter count is recalculated and displayed in real-time.

### Training

Training runs in a `requestAnimationFrame` loop, not in a blocking loop. Each frame executes a configurable number of training steps (1–20, default 5) via `model.trainOnBatch()`. This keeps the UI responsive during training — the visualization continues to render at 60fps while the model learns.

Tensor disposal is critical here. Every `trainOnBatch` call returns a loss tensor that must be disposed manually. The input and label tensors are created once when training starts and disposed when training stops. The `getActivations` method wraps all intermediate tensors in `tf.tidy()` to prevent leaks.

The stats bar at the bottom of the screen shows live training status: whether the model is training or idle, the current epoch count, the latest loss value, and the total parameter count.

### Data Generation

The `DataGenerator` class provides three classic 2D classification problems:

- **XOR** — 200 random points in [-1, 1]² labeled by the XOR of their sign. A two-class problem that requires at least one hidden layer to solve.
- **Circle** — 200 random points labeled 1 if inside a circle of radius √0.5, 0 otherwise. A non-linear boundary that tests the network's ability to learn curved decision surfaces.
- **Spiral** — 300 points arranged in two interleaving spirals. The hardest of the three — requires a network with sufficient capacity and the right activation function to separate the classes.

Each dataset returns `{ data, labels, inputDim }` and is passed directly to TensorFlow.js tensors for training.

### Canvas Rendering

![Network visualization with decision boundary overlay and signal particles](/images/projects/expecto-network.png)

The `Renderer` class manages two stacked canvases: one for the network visualization and one for the decision boundary overlay. Both are rendered at device pixel ratio for sharp display on high-DPI screens.

The network canvas draws, in order:

1. **Grid** — A subtle dot grid at 40px intervals for visual texture.
2. **Connections** — Lines between every neuron in adjacent layers. Line thickness and opacity scale with the absolute weight value. Positive weights are teal (`#6f9d9a`), negative weights are salmon (`#d98b73`).
3. **Signals** — Animated particles that travel along connections. Each frame, random signal pairs are spawned between adjacent layers. The particle color matches the sign of the source neuron's activation. Signals accelerate as they travel (eased progress curve) and fade out at the midpoint.
4. **Neurons** — Circles filled with dark (`#292524`) and stroked with a color that indicates their role: mustard (`#d6b347`) for input neurons, teal for hidden neurons, salmon for output neurons. Stroke alpha scales with the absolute activation value, so neurons "light up" when they're active. Each neuron displays its activation value (or label for input/output neurons). A radial glow gradient behind each neuron provides additional visual feedback.
5. **Layer labels** — "Input", "Hidden 1", "Hidden 2", ..., "Output" positioned below each layer column.

### Interactivity

Neurons are draggable. Click and drag any neuron to reposition it. The renderer tracks the drag offset and constrains the neuron to the canvas bounds. On touch devices, touch events are handled separately from mouse events with the same drag logic.

Clicking an input neuron toggles its value between 0 and 1. This triggers a forward pass through the network with the new input, updates all neuron activations, and spawns a burst of signal particles to visualize the data flowing through the network.

A tooltip follows the cursor on hover, showing the neuron's layer, index, and current activation value (e.g., "L1·N2 | activation: +0.3842").

### Decision Boundary

The decision boundary is a separate canvas layer rendered at reduced resolution (60×60 grid). For each grid cell, the network predicts the output for the corresponding (x, y) coordinate in [-1, 1]². The cell is colored by interpolating between salmon (prediction = 0) and teal (prediction = 1) at 18% opacity, composited with screen blend mode.

The boundary updates every 10 training steps during active training. This is the most expensive operation in the app — 3,600 forward passes per update — which is why it's rendered at low resolution and throttled during training.

## Technology

The entire app runs client-side in the browser. No server, no backend, no build step. Three files, loaded directly.

### TensorFlow.js

TensorFlow.js (v4.17.0, loaded from CDN) provides the neural network primitives. The app uses `tf.sequential()` to build dense feed-forward networks, `model.trainOnBatch()` for single-batch gradient descent, and `model.predict()` for inference. Weights are extracted via `layer.getWeights()` after each training step for visualization.

Tensor management is the most error-prone part of the integration. The app creates tensors for training data once and disposes them on training stop. Intermediate tensors from `getActivations()` are wrapped in `tf.tidy()`. Loss tensors from `trainOnBatch()` are disposed immediately after reading their value.

### Canvas API

All rendering uses the Canvas 2D API. The network visualization canvas handles neuron circles, connection lines, signal particles, and layer labels. The boundary canvas is a separate element layered on top with CSS `mix-blend-mode: screen` and 30% opacity.

Canvas dimensions are scaled by `window.devicePixelRatio` for crisp rendering on retina displays. The canvas is resized on window resize events, and the neuron layout is recalculated to fill the available space.

Animation runs in a `requestAnimationFrame` loop that calls `renderer.render()` every frame, clearing and redrawing the entire network visualization. Signal particles are updated and pruned each frame — expired signals (progress ≥ 1) are filtered out.

### Drag and Drop

Neuron dragging is implemented with pointer math on `mousedown`, `mousemove`, and `mouseup` events. Touch equivalents (`touchstart`, `touchmove`, `touchend`) are handled separately with `preventDefault()` to suppress scroll. The drag offset is calculated from the click position relative to the neuron center, and the neuron position is clamped to canvas bounds during the drag.

The app distinguishes between a click and a drag using a `hasDragged` flag. If the mouse moves during a mousedown-mouseup cycle, the click handler is suppressed. This prevents accidental input toggles when the user intended to drag.

## UI Design

![Control panel with network architecture controls, training settings, and display toggles](/images/projects/expecto-controls.png)

The interface is a single-screen layout with no scrolling. A left sidebar (300px) contains all controls, organized into three collapsible sections: Network, Training, and Display. The main area is the canvas. A stats bar at the bottom shows live training metrics.

The visual language is warm and tactile. The color palette is built around dark warm greys (`#1a1816`, `#211e1b`, `#292524`) with four accent colors: mustard (`#d6b347`) for primary actions and input neurons, teal (`#6f9d9a`) for positive values and hidden neurons, salmon (`#d98b73`) for negative values and output neurons, and slime (`#9fb06f`) for active/training states.

Buttons have tactile box shadows (`4px 4px 0px 0px #463f3a`) that compress on click, giving a physical "press" feel. Panel sections have subtle rotations (`±0.2deg`) for a hand-assembled aesthetic. A noise texture overlay at 3.5% opacity adds grain across the entire interface. The header and control panel section headings use Fraunces, a variable optical size serif, while body text uses Inter.

A decorative squiggle SVG divider separates the header from the main content. Navigation links in the header rotate slightly on hover.

The UI is optimized for desktop but degrades gracefully on mobile: the control panel stacks above the canvas, nav links are hidden, and panel sections arrange horizontally with a minimum width of 220px.

## What I Learned

- TensorFlow.js is surprisingly capable for browser-based ML, but tensor disposal is a constant concern. Every `trainOnBatch`, `predict`, and `getActivations` call creates tensors that must be explicitly disposed or wrapped in `tf.tidy()`. Miss one and you have a memory leak that compounds over thousands of training steps.
- Canvas 2D is the right tool for this kind of visualization. WebGL would be faster for thousands of neurons, but at the scale of a toy neural network (2–12 neurons per layer), Canvas 2D is plenty fast and much simpler to work with. No shaders, no buffers, no coordinate transforms — just `beginPath`, `arc`, `stroke`.
- The decision boundary is the most expensive thing to render. 3,600 forward passes at 60×60 resolution, each running through the full TensorFlow.js model. Throttling it to every 10 training steps and rendering at low resolution keeps it interactive, but it's still the bottleneck.
- Making neurons draggable sounds trivial until you need to handle touch events, distinguish clicks from drags, and constrain positions to canvas bounds. The `hasDragged` flag pattern (set on first mousemove, checked in click handler) is simple but essential.
- The warm, tactile UI style — tactile shadows, noise overlays, slight rotations, serif headings — works well for a tool that's meant to feel approachable rather than clinical. It makes a neural network sandbox feel like a toy, which is the point.

## Challenges

- **Tensor lifecycle**: Managing TensorFlow.js tensor disposal across training loops, forward passes, and architecture changes required careful `tf.tidy()` wrapping and explicit `dispose()` calls. A single missed disposal during `getActivations` would leak hundreds of tensors per frame.
- **Decision boundary performance**: Rendering 3,600 predictions per boundary update is expensive. The solution was low-resolution grid (60×60), throttled updates (every 10 training steps), and a separate canvas layer with screen blend mode to avoid recompositing the network visualization.
- **Architecture mutations**: Adding or removing layers or neurons requires disposing the old model, rebuilding from the new architecture array, re-extracting weights, and relaying out neurons — all while preserving the positions of neurons that still exist. The `existingPositions` map passed to `layoutNetwork` handles this by matching neurons by their `layer-index` key.
- **Touch vs. mouse**: Mobile touch events don't map cleanly to mouse events. `touchstart` doesn't fire a `click`, `touchmove` doesn't have `clientX` directly (it's on `e.touches[0]`), and `preventDefault()` is needed to suppress scroll during drag. The app handles both input paths separately.
- **60fps rendering loop**: Drawing connections (N×M lines per layer pair), signal particles, neuron glows, and layer labels every frame at 60fps requires keeping the draw calls simple. No canvas shadows (too expensive), no gradients on connections (only on neurons), and signal particles are filtered out immediately when they expire.

document.title: JIWW — Jump In, Water's Wet
document.description: A web app that generates complete, production-ready MIDI song arrangements from a single genre selection — full multi-track songs with hooks, verses, choruses, and dense musical layering.

body.raw:
## Overview

JIWW (Jump In, Water's Wet) is a web app that generates complete, production-ready MIDI song arrangements from a single genre selection. You pick one of 16 genres — house, techno, drum & bass, hip-hop, lo-fi, synthwave, jazz fusion, and more — click "Jump In," and the app creates a full multi-track MIDI song with tempo, swing, instrument palette, arrangement structure, melodic hooks, verses, choruses, and dense musical layering.

The output is designed to be immediately usable. Import the downloaded MIDI stems into any DAW and you'll hear a structured, genre-appropriate song idea — not a random loop, not a single pattern, but a complete arrangement with intro, verses, choruses, breakdown, and outro. Each track uses General MIDI-compatible instruments, so playback works out of the box.

![JIWW landing page with genre selector and generate button](/images/projects/jiww-header.png)

## The Problem It Solves

Most MIDI generation tools produce loops. You get a 4-bar drum pattern or a chord progression, and you're left to arrange, layer, and develop it yourself. That's useful, but it's not a song. The gap between a generated loop and a full arrangement is hours of manual work — choosing instruments, writing basslines, building transitions, adding hooks, varying sections, and managing density across verses and choruses.

JIWW fills that gap. It generates complete songs: 8 to 16 tracks, 64 to 128 bars, with distinct sections that build and release energy. The arrangement has a beginning, middle, and end. The chorus has a hook. The bassline moves. The drums have fills and transitions. It's a song sketch, not a building block.

The target user is a music producer who wants a quick starting point. They don't need to make every creative decision from scratch — they need something structured enough to react to, modify, and develop into a finished track.

## Supported Genres

JIWW supports 16 genres at launch, each with a dedicated generation profile that controls BPM, swing, instrumentation, arrangement style, harmonic complexity, and rhythmic feel:

1. **House** — 118–128 BPM, four-on-the-floor grooves, piano chords, plucks, and synth stabs
2. **Techno** — 124–140 BPM, minimal percussion, hypnotic arpeggios, dark pads
3. **Drum & Bass** — 160–176 BPM, breakbeat drums, reese bass, atmospheric textures
4. **UK Garage** — 128–138 BPM, shuffled rhythms, organ stabs, vocal chops
5. **Hip-Hop** — 80–100 BPM, boom-bap drums, sampled-style melodies, vinyl textures
6. **Trap** — 130–150 BPM, 808 bass, hi-hat rolls, dark melodies
7. **Lo-Fi Hip-Hop** — 68–92 BPM, dusty drums, electric piano, heavy swing, vinyl crackle
8. **Synthwave** — 84–112 BPM, analog synth bass, arpeggiated sequences, warm pads
9. **Ambient** — 60–90 BPM, evolving textures, sparse percussion, long sustains
10. **Funk** — 90–115 BPM, slap bass, clavinet, brass, tight drum grooves
11. **Disco** — 110–125 BPM, four-on-the-floor, string arrangements, wah guitar
12. **Pop** — 95–125 BPM, polished hooks, synth layers, clean arrangements
13. **Indie Rock** — 95–145 BPM, guitar-driven, energetic drums, melodic bass
14. **Reggaeton** — 85–105 BPM, dembow rhythm, Latin percussion, synth melodies
15. **Afrobeat** — 95–120 BPM, polyrhythmic percussion, horn sections, guitar interplay
16. **Jazz Fusion** — 90–140 BPM, electric piano, complex harmony, ride-heavy drums

Each genre card on the landing page shows the genre name, approximate BPM range, and a short groove description to help users pick quickly.

## How It Works

### Generation Pipeline

The generation engine is a modular pipeline that runs deterministically from a seed. Given the same genre and seed, it produces the same song every time. The pipeline has 14 stages:

1. **Genre profile selection** — Load the genre's BPM range, swing range, instrument pools, chord templates, drum templates, and arrangement templates.
2. **Global song parameters** — Choose BPM, swing amount, key, scale, time signature, and song length from the genre's allowed ranges.
3. **Arrangement generation** — Build the song structure: intro, verse A, chorus A, verse B, chorus B, bridge, final chorus, outro. Each section has a bar count and energy level.
4. **Track selection** — Choose 4 to 16 instruments from the genre's instrument pool, ensuring at least one rhythm, one bass, one harmonic, and one melodic track.
5. **Harmony generation** — Create chord progressions for the song, with section-level variation.
6. **Rhythm foundation** — Generate drum and percussion patterns with genre-specific groove, fills, and transitions.
7. **Bass generation** — Create basslines with root movement, syncopation, and section-specific intensity.
8. **Chord track generation** — Render chord voicings with inversions, rhythmic comping, and density changes.
9. **Melodic hook generation** — Create at least one memorable hook using motif logic: ascending/descending contours, call-and-response phrases, repetition with variation.
10. **Supporting parts** — Generate counter-melodies, pads, arpeggios, and texture layers.
11. **Transitions and fills** — Add section transitions, risers, and drum fills.
12. **Humanization** — Apply velocity variation and micro-timing offsets for a natural feel.
13. **MIDI rendering** — Convert the internal representation to General MIDI-compatible `.mid` files.
14. **ZIP packaging** — Bundle all tracks with metadata into a downloadable ZIP.

### Song Structure

Every generated song uses a complete arrangement template. The minimum structure:

- **Intro** — Low energy, sparse instrumentation, sets the tonal palette
- **Verse A** — Moderate density, space for vocals or lead development
- **Chorus A** — Full hook, more active tracks, wider voicings
- **Verse B** — Variation on Verse A, slight density increase
- **Chorus B** — Variation on Chorus A, additional layers
- **Bridge / Breakdown** — Energy drop, harmonic departure, textural shift
- **Final Chorus** — Maximum density, all tracks active, widest register
- **Outro** — Energy wind-down, sparse ending

Electronic genres get longer intros and outros (8–16 bars). Pop and hip-hop genres use more compact arrangements (4–8 bar sections).

### Track Generation

Each song contains 4 to 16 instrument tracks drawn from genre-specific pools. Every track has a role:

- **Drums** — Kick, snare, hats, percussion, fills, transitions
- **Bass** — Root movement, syncopation, octave variation
- **Chords** — Voicings, inversions, rhythmic comping
- **Melody / Hook** — Motifs, call-and-response, repetition with variation
- **Counter-melody** — Complementary phrases that respond to the hook
- **Pad** — Sustained harmonies, textural support
- **Arpeggio** — Rhythmic note patterns from the chord tones
- **FX** — Risers, hits, transitions, atmosphere

Every track includes section-level participation — tracks mute and unmute across the arrangement to create density variation between verses and choruses.

### MIDI Quality

Generated MIDI is designed to be musically useful, not random. Each track includes:

- Humanized velocity (controlled random variation, not uniform)
- Humanized timing (micro-offsets within genre-appropriate limits)
- Genre-correct rhythm patterns
- Section-level variation (verse patterns differ from chorus patterns)
- Repetition with small changes (motifs evolve, not loop exactly)
- Harmonic alignment with the chord progression
- Proper bar and beat alignment

Melodic tracks use motif logic: a short rhythmic and melodic cell that repeats, varies, and develops across sections. Hooks appear in choruses and are hinted at before the first chorus.

## Technology

JIWW is built for Vercel Serverless. The entire generation pipeline runs synchronously within a serverless API route — no background workers, no persistent compute, no long-running processes.

### Stack

- **Next.js** with React for the frontend and API routes
- **TypeScript** for type-safe generation logic
- **Tailwind CSS** for styling
- **Web Audio API** with General MIDI soundfont for browser-based preview playback
- **JSZip** for in-memory ZIP generation
- **Custom MIDI renderer** for generating `.mid` files from internal representation

### Serverless Design

The generation engine is designed around serverless constraints:

- All MIDI files are generated in memory — no filesystem writes
- ZIP files are generated in memory using JSZip
- Generation completes within typical Vercel serverless execution limits
- Songs are kept between 64 and 128 bars to control output size
- Tracks are capped at 16 to limit processing time
- The generation pipeline is deterministic and efficient — same seed produces same output

For the MVP, the frontend may encode the generated song payload client-side and generate download files in the browser, avoiding the need for persistent server-side storage.

### Deterministic Generation

Every song is generated from a seed. The seed can be user-provided or auto-generated. Given the same genre and seed, the pipeline produces identical output every time. This means users can share seeds to reproduce specific songs.

### Preview Playback

The app uses the Web Audio API with a General MIDI soundfont loaded on demand. Users can preview the full song (all tracks together) or solo-preview individual tracks. Playback controls include play, pause, stop, restart, timeline position, and BPM display.

Soundfonts are lazy-loaded to keep the initial page load fast.

## UI Design

The UI is designed to feel whimsical, casual, and low-pressure. The visual language uses soft aquatic colours, rounded cards, playful typography, gentle gradients, and subtle wave motifs. The tone communicates: "Don't overthink it — jump in."

### Color Palette

- Pool Blue: `#6ED8FF`
- Deep Teal: `#087E8B`
- Foam White: `#F7FDFF`
- Coral Pop: `#FF7A6B`
- Sunny Yellow: `#FFD166`
- Lilac Mist: `#CDB4DB`
- Ink Navy: `#103040`

### Landing Page

The landing page is a single screen with the app name, subtitle, genre selector, and generate button. Genre cards are visually fun and scannable — each shows the genre name, BPM range, and a short groove description. An optional advanced settings accordion lets users specify seed, track count, BPM, swing level, and song length.

### Loading Modal

After clicking "Jump In," a loading modal appears with playful stage messages:

1. "Picking a tempo that can swim…"
2. "Teaching the bassline to float…"
3. "Sprinkling hooks into the chorus…"
4. "Making the drums less awkward…"
5. "Packing your MIDI suitcase…"

A progress indicator and animated visual element keep the user informed while the generation runs.

### Results Screen

The results screen shows the generated song metadata (genre, BPM, key, scale, swing, track count, arrangement length), a full-song player, a track list with per-track players, and download buttons. Each track panel displays the track name, role, instrument, MIDI channel, and section activity indicators.

Download options include individual MIDI tracks, a full-arrangement MIDI file, and a ZIP bundle containing all tracks plus metadata.

### Microcopy

The UI copy maintains the casual, playful tone throughout:

- Primary CTA: "Jump In"
- Regenerate: "Generate Another Splash"
- ZIP download: "Download the Whole Pool"
- Track download: "Grab this track"
- Loading: "The water's getting musical..."
- Result: "Your song just cannonballed into existence."
- Error: "The song slipped on the pool tiles. Try generating again."

## What I Learned

- Deterministic generation from a seed is essential for reproducibility, but getting musical randomness to feel natural within deterministic constraints is the hard part. The seed controls everything, but the output shouldn't feel robotic.
- General MIDI is the right choice for preview and export. It's universally compatible with DAWs, sounds acceptable for preview, and keeps the output format simple. No audio rendering, no sample libraries, no format conversion.
- Serverless constraints force good engineering. Generating everything in memory, capping song length, limiting track count, and avoiding filesystem writes all lead to a cleaner, more portable architecture.
- The arrangement is what makes the output feel like a song, not a loop. Without distinct sections — verse, chorus, bridge — with different energy levels and track participation, the output would just be a long repeating pattern.
- Hook generation using motif logic (short cells that repeat, vary, and develop) produces more musical results than random note generation. The hook needs to be rhythmically distinct from the bass and chord parts, resolve against the harmony, and repeat enough to be memorable.

## Challenges

- **Musical quality within constraints**: Generating MIDI that sounds like a human wrote it — with appropriate rhythm, harmony, melody, and arrangement — while running entirely in a serverless function with no external AI services.
- **Genre differentiation**: Making 16 genres feel genuinely different required deep genre-specific profiles with distinct BPM ranges, swing amounts, instrument pools, chord templates, drum patterns, and arrangement styles.
- **Serverless execution limits**: Keeping generation fast enough to complete within Vercel's serverless timeout while producing 64–128 bars of 4–16 tracks of MIDI data.
- **Preview performance**: Loading General MIDI soundfonts in the browser and playing back multi-track arrangements in sync without blocking the UI thread.
- **Arrangement density**: Balancing track count, section energy, and layering so that verses feel sparse and choruses feel full without the output sounding cluttered or empty.

document.title: Knut Counter
document.description: An AI usage cost tracking and optimization tool that monitors spending across 43+ providers, benchmarks models, and recommends the best model for your task based on cost, quality, and budget constraints.

body.raw:
## Overview

Knut Counter is a full-stack AI usage cost tracking and optimization tool. It aggregates spending data from over 43 AI providers — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, OpenRouter, xAI, DeepSeek, and more — into a unified dashboard with monthly spend projections, token usage breakdowns, and per-provider sparkline charts.

Beyond tracking, Knut Counter is an optimization tool. Its recommendation engine suggests the best model for a given task by weighing cost against quality using dual benchmark sources. A quality-vs-cost slider lets users tune recommendations to their budget, returning the cheapest viable option, the highest quality option, or the best balance between the two.

The name comes from the wizarding world's smallest unit of currency — a reminder that every token has a cost, and small optimizations add up.

![Knut Counter dashboard showing aggregated spend, token usage breakdowns, and per-provider sparkline charts](/images/projects/knutcounter_dashboard.png)

## The Problem It Solves

Using AI APIs across multiple providers is a billing nightmare. Each provider has different pricing tiers, different token counting methods, and different dashboard interfaces. OpenAI shows one thing, Anthropic shows another, and OpenRouter shows a third. There's no single place to see total AI spend, compare costs across providers, or figure out which model actually gives the best value for a specific task.

Developers and teams using multiple AI providers end up with:
- Scattered billing data across a dozen dashboards
- No visibility into which models are driving costs
- No way to compare price-to-quality ratios across providers
- Manual effort to track budgets and detect anomalies
- Guesswork when choosing between models

Knut Counter solves all of this. One dashboard. One budget. One recommendation engine.

## Features

### Unified Dashboard

The dashboard aggregates data from all connected providers into a single view. Monthly spend is calculated by summing usage records across providers, with projected costs extrapolated from the current month's pace. Token usage is broken down by provider and model, with sparkline charts showing daily trends.

The dashboard also surfaces alerts: budget thresholds at 50%, 75%, 90%, and 100%; stale sync warnings when a provider hasn't reported data recently; unusual spend spikes; and missing pricing data notifications.

### Model Recommendations

![Model comparison view showing cost-to-quality analysis across providers](/images/projects/knutcounter_compare.png)

The recommendation engine is the core differentiator. Given a task type (e.g., "code generation," "summarization," "general chat"), it returns three picks:

| Pick | Strategy |
|------|----------|
| **Cheapest** | Lowest cost that meets a minimum quality threshold |
| **Best Quality** | Highest benchmark score regardless of cost |
| **Best Balance** | Optimal cost-to-quality ratio using a weighted score |

The quality-vs-cost slider adjusts the weighting. Slide toward quality and the engine favors models with higher intelligence and coding benchmarks. Slide toward cost and it favors cheaper models that still pass the minimum bar.

Scores are computed using benchmark data from two sources:

| Source | Metrics |
|--------|---------|
| **Artificial Analysis** | Intelligence index, coding ability, math reasoning |
| **BenchLM** | Leaderboard rankings across standardized tasks |

### Models Table

![Models table ranking top models by usage with cost, benchmark, and speed columns](/images/projects/knutcounter_models.png)

A ranked table of the top 100 models by weekly usage, with columns for:

- Input/output cost per million tokens
- Intelligence benchmark score
- Coding ability benchmark
- Speed (tokens per second)
- Context window size
- Provider

Sortable by any column. Filterable by provider and capability.

### Multi-Provider Support

43+ providers are supported through three connection methods:

| Method | How It Works |
|--------|--------------|
| **API Key** | Knut Counter calls the provider's usage API directly |
| **Manual Tracking** | Users log usage records manually |
| **CSV/JSON Import** | Upload billing exports from provider dashboards |

API keys are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM. The encrypted keys are used server-side to sync usage data — they're never returned to the client.

### Currency Conversion

All costs are stored in USD but displayed in the user's preferred currency. Real-time exchange rates are fetched from Open Exchange Rates with fallback to Frankfurter. Over 50 currencies are supported.

## Architecture

Knut Counter is a monorepo managed by pnpm workspaces and Turborepo. The architecture separates concerns into distinct packages:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Vercel                                │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │              Serverless Functions (api/)               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐    │  │
│  │  │Dashboard│ │Recommend│ │ Provider│ │ Alerts  │    │  │
│  │  │  Data   │ │ Engine  │ │  Sync   │ │Evaluator│    │  │
│  │  └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘    │  │
│  └───────┼───────────┼───────────┼───────────┼──────────┘  │
│          │           │           │           │              │
│  ┌───────┴───────────┴───────────┴───────────┴──────────┐  │
│  │                    Packages                           │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐   │  │
│  │  │  @knut  │ │  @knut  │ │  @knut  │ │  @knut  │   │  │
│  │  │   db    │ │ pricing │ │providers│ │  shared │   │  │
│  │  └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘   │  │
│  └───────┼───────────┼───────────┼───────────┼──────────┘  │
│          │           │           │           │              │
│  ┌───────┴───────────┴───────────┴───────────┴──────────┐  │
│  │                 Supabase / PostgreSQL                  │  │
│  │  users │ provider_accounts │ usage_records │ alerts   │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          ▲
          │
┌─────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Client (Expo)                             │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  React Native + expo-router + react-native-web        │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐   │  │
│  │  │Dashboard│ │ Models  │ │Recommend│ │ Settings│   │  │
│  │  │  Page   │ │  Table  │ │  Page   │ │  Page   │   │  │
│  │  └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘   │  │
│  │                   Zustand State                       │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                             │
│  Works on: Web (PWA), iOS, Android                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Frontend

The client is built with React Native and Expo SDK 53, using expo-router for file-based routing. The same codebase runs on web (as a PWA via react-native-web and Metro bundler), iOS, and Android. Zustand manages client-side state, with Supabase handling authentication and real-time subscriptions.

The UI uses a custom component library in `packages/ui` with Tailwind CSS styling. Components are designed for React Native compatibility — they render correctly on both web and mobile without platform-specific code.

### Backend

The API layer runs on Vercel Serverless Functions. Each route is a standalone function in `api/` that handles a specific domain: dashboard aggregation, model recommendations, provider sync, alert evaluation, credential management.

Serverless functions validate Supabase JWTs via the service role key, then perform operations scoped to the authenticated user's ID. No function can access another user's data.

### Database

PostgreSQL on Supabase with Drizzle ORM 0.43 managing the schema. Eight tables handle the full domain model:

| Table | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `users` | Profiles, timezone, currency preference, monthly budget |
| `provider_registry` | Master list of 43+ AI providers |
| `provider_accounts` | Per-user connected providers with encrypted API keys |
| `usage_records` | Individual usage events with tokens, cost, model |
| `usage_caps` | Credit balance and usage cap tracking |
| `pricing_snapshots` | Historical pricing from multiple sources |
| `model_benchmark_snapshots` | Intelligence, coding, math, speed benchmarks |
| `alerts` | Budget, stale sync, and anomaly alerts |
| `import_jobs` | CSV/JSON import job tracking |

Drizzle generates type-safe queries and handles migrations. The schema is defined once in `packages/db` and shared across all API routes.

### Data Pipeline

Pricing and benchmark data flows through a multi-source pipeline:

```
External APIs                    Knut Counter                     Database
─────────────                    ────────────                     ────────

OpenRouter API ─────────┐
Artificial Analysis ─────┤
LiteLLM (GitHub JSON) ──┼──→ packages/pricing ──→ pricing_snapshots
models.dev API ─────────┤      (normalize,
BenchLM API ────────────┘       dedupe, score)

Provider Usage APIs ──────────→ api/providers/sync ──→ usage_records
                                                          │
Open Exchange Rates ──────────→ packages/shared ──→ (convert on read)
```

## Credential Security

API keys are the most sensitive data in the system. Knut Counter treats them accordingly:

1. **Encryption at rest** — Keys are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a key derived from `CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` via scrypt. The derived key never leaves the server.

2. **Never exposed to client** — API responses include a `hasCredentials: boolean` flag, never the raw key. Even if the database is compromised, encrypted keys are useless without the encryption key.

3. **Scoped access** — Every API route validates the Supabase JWT and scopes database queries to the authenticated user's ID. No route can access another user's credentials or data.

4. **Sync-only usage** — Encrypted keys are decrypted only during server-side sync operations, when the server calls the provider's API to fetch usage data. The keys exist in memory only for the duration of that request.

## Design Decisions

### React Native + Expo for a Dashboard App

React Native is an unusual choice for a dashboard-heavy analytics tool. The decision came from wanting a single codebase that works on web and mobile. The dashboard is the primary interface on desktop, but mobile is where quick budget checks and alert responses happen.

expo-router handles file-based routing identically to Next.js, and react-native-web bridges the component gap. The custom UI package in `packages/ui` contains components that render correctly on both platforms without conditional rendering.

The tradeoff is that complex data visualizations (sparkline charts, benchmark comparisons) require custom implementations rather than using web-only charting libraries. Knut Counter uses lightweight Canvas-based sparklines that work on both platforms.

### Drizzle ORM Over Prisma

Drizzle was chosen for three reasons: it generates SQL that looks hand-written, it has zero runtime dependencies beyond the database driver, and its schema definitions are plain TypeScript objects that can be shared across packages without code generation steps.

The type safety is comparable to Prisma, but the query builder stays closer to SQL. This matters for the aggregation queries that power the dashboard — raw SQL patterns like window functions and CTEs are easier to express in Drizzle's builder.

### Monorepo with Turborepo

The separation into `packages/db`, `packages/pricing`, `packages/providers`, `packages/shared`, and `packages/ui` reflects the natural boundaries of the system. The pricing package can be tested independently. The provider connectors can be added without touching the database layer. The shared types package ensures the client and server agree on data shapes.

Turborepo handles the build graph, caching, and parallel execution. A change to `packages/db` triggers a rebuild of all API routes that depend on it, but leaves the UI package untouched.

### Supabase for Auth and Database

Supabase provides two things that would be painful to build from scratch: a hosted PostgreSQL database with connection pooling, and a battle-tested authentication system with JWT tokens. The free tier is sufficient for development and small-scale production.

The service role key validates JWTs server-side without calling Supabase's auth API on every request — the key is used to verify the JWT signature locally. This keeps API latency low and removes Supabase as a dependency on the critical path.

## What I Learned

- **Encryption in serverless is nuanced.** AES-256-GCM requires a unique nonce per encryption operation. Reusing a nonce with the same key breaks the security guarantees. Knut Counter generates a random 12-byte nonce for each encryption and stores it alongside the ciphertext. The encryption key itself is derived once from the environment variable using scrypt and cached in the serverless function's module scope.

- **Multi-source data normalization is hard.** Different pricing APIs report costs in different units (per token, per 1K tokens, per 1M tokens), with different rounding, and sometimes with different definitions of what counts as a "token." The pricing package normalizes everything to cost-per-million-tokens and uses confidence scores to weight conflicting sources.

- **React Native web compatibility has limits.** Some React Native primitives (`ScrollView`, `FlatList`) behave differently on web than expected. The solution was to use a thin abstraction layer in `packages/ui` that picks the right implementation based on the platform, while keeping the component API identical.

- **Serverless cold starts affect UX.** Vercel functions cold-start in 200-500ms, which adds noticeable latency to API calls. The mitigation is aggressive client-side caching with Zustand, optimistic UI updates, and background data refresh on a schedule.

- **Monorepo tooling has a learning curve.** pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, and shared TypeScript configs each have their own conventions and pitfalls. The initial setup took longer than expected, but the payoff in code reuse and type safety across packages has been significant.

## Challenges

- **Provider API inconsistency.** Every provider's usage API returns data in a different format, with different time granularities, different token counting methods, and different rate limits. The provider connectors in `packages/providers` handle this by normalizing each provider's response into a common `UsageRecord` shape. Adding a new provider means writing a new connector, but the interface is well-defined.

- **Benchmark data freshness.** Model benchmarks change as providers release new model versions. The benchmark snapshot system stores historical data with timestamps, so the recommendation engine can weight recent benchmarks more heavily. Stale benchmarks are flagged for refresh.

- **Budget alert accuracy.** Projected costs assume current usage pace continues through the month. This is reasonable for steady workloads but inaccurate for bursty usage. The alert system uses conservative thresholds (50%, 75%, 90%, 100%) and includes a "stale sync" warning when data is too old to project reliably.

- **Mobile web performance.** The dashboard loads a lot of data — provider summaries, usage charts, model tables, alerts. On mobile web, this can cause layout shifts and slow renders. The solution was lazy loading, virtualized lists for the models table, and skeleton placeholders during data fetches.

## Cron Jobs

For automated data collection, Knut Counter supports scheduled jobs via Vercel Cron:

| Schedule | Endpoint | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Daily 3am | `/api/pricing/refresh` | Refresh pricing from all sources |
| Hourly | `/api/providers/sync` | Sync usage data from connected providers |
| Every 30 min | `/api/alerts/evaluate` | Evaluate and fire budget/stale alerts |

These jobs run independently of user requests, keeping the dashboard data fresh without requiring manual syncs.

## Future Work

- **Team workspaces** — Shared budgets and usage visibility across team members
- **Cost allocation tags** — Tag usage records with project or team labels for cost attribution
- **Slack/Discord alerts** — Push budget alerts to team communication channels
- **Historical trends** — Month-over-month comparisons and year-over-year analysis
- **Custom benchmark sources** — Allow users to add their own benchmark data
- **API access** — REST API for programmatic access to usage and recommendation data

`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["expo-router handles file-based routing identically to Next.js, and react-native-web bridges the component gap. `,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"The tradeoff is that complex data visualizations (sparkline charts, benchmark comparisons) require custom implementations rather than using web-only charting libraries. Knut Counter uses lightweight Canvas-based sparklines that work on both platforms."}),`

`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Drizzle was chosen for three reasons: it generates SQL that looks hand-written, it has zero runtime dependencies beyond the database driver, and its schema definitions are plain TypeScript objects that can be shared across packages without code generation steps."}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"The type safety is comparable to Prisma, but the query builder stays closer to SQL. This matters for the aggregation queries that power the dashboard \u2014 raw SQL patterns like window functions and CTEs are easier to express in Drizzle's builder."}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.h3,{id:"monorepo-with-turborepo",children:(0,n.jsx)(e.a,{href:"#monorepo-with-turborepo",children:"Monorepo with Turborepo"})}),`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["The separation into ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/db"}),", ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/pricing"}),", ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/providers"}),", ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/shared"}),", and ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/ui"})," reflects the natural boundaries of the system. The pricing package can be tested independently. The provider connectors can be added without touching the database layer. The shared types package ensures the client and server agree on data shapes."]}),`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["Turborepo handles the build graph, caching, and parallel execution. A change to ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/db"})," triggers a rebuild of all API routes that depend on it, but leaves the UI package untouched."]}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.h3,{id:"supabase-for-auth-and-database",children:(0,n.jsx)(e.a,{href:"#supabase-for-auth-and-database",children:"Supabase for Auth and Database"})}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Supabase provides two things that would be painful to build from scratch: a hosted PostgreSQL database with connection pooling, and a battle-tested authentication system with JWT tokens. The free tier is sufficient for development and small-scale production."}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"The service role key validates JWTs server-side without calling Supabase's auth API on every request \u2014 the key is used to verify the JWT signature locally. This keeps API latency low and removes Supabase as a dependency on the critical path."}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.h2,{id:"what-i-learned",children:(0,n.jsx)(e.a,{href:"#what-i-learned",children:"What I Learned"})}),`

`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[(0,n.jsx)(e.strong,{children:"Encryption in serverless is nuanced."})," AES-256-GCM requires a unique nonce per encryption operation. Reusing a nonce with the same key breaks the security guarantees. Knut Counter generates a random 12-byte nonce for each encryption and stores it alongside the ciphertext. The encryption key itself is derived once from the environment variable using scrypt and cached in the serverless function's module scope."]}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.li,{children:[`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[(0,n.jsx)(e.strong,{children:"Multi-source data normalization is hard."}),' Different pricing APIs report costs in different units (per token, per 1K tokens, per 1M tokens), with different rounding, and sometimes with different definitions of what counts as a "token." The pricing package normalizes everything to cost-per-million-tokens and uses confidence scores to weight conflicting sources.']}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.li,{children:[`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[(0,n.jsx)(e.strong,{children:"React Native web compatibility has limits."})," Some React Native primitives (",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"ScrollView"}),", ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"FlatList"}),") behave differently on web than expected. The solution was to use a thin abstraction layer in ",(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{children:"packages/ui"})," that picks the right implementation based on the platform, while keeping the component API identical."]}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.li,{children:[`
`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[(0,n.jsx)(e.strong,{children:"Serverless cold starts affect UX."})," Vercel functions cold-start in 200-500ms, which adds noticeable latency to API calls. The mitigation is aggressive client-side caching with Zustand, optimistic UI updates, and background data refresh on a schedule."]}),`
`]}),`

`]}),`
