document.title: Melodestiny
document.description: A web-based song lyric analysis tool that scores pop songs on structural and lyrical quality using 25 algorithmic techniques derived from the playbooks of the world's most successful pop songwriters.

body.raw:
## Overview

Melodestiny is a web-based song lyric analysis tool that scores pop songs on structural and lyrical quality. You paste in a song title and lyrics, hit Analyze, and get a weighted score out of 100 with a detailed breakdown across 25 algorithmic techniques — each one derived from the playbooks of legendary songwriters like Max Martin, Diane Warren, Jack Antonoff, Pharrell Williams, Julia Michaels, Ed Sheeran, Ryan Tedder, Sia, Benny Blanco, Ilsey Juber, and Linda Perry.

The results include a wax-seal-style score badge, an interactive technique score grid, a collapsible breakdown table, highlighted lyrics with inline annotations, syllable boundary markers, and hoverable line-level feedback badges. It also offers client-side AI lyric generation using OpenRouter, and triggers to export raw lyrics or detailed feedback reports. The entire analysis runs client-side in the browser via a Rust-compiled WebAssembly module — no server, no API calls, no latency.

The design is intentionally anachronistic: parchment backgrounds, serif typography featuring the calligraphic display font **Mustopha**, muted earth tones, and hand-drawn-style dividers. The idea is that analyzing lyrics should feel like opening a well-loved leather journal in a Victorian songwriter's study, not staring at a dashboard.

![Melodestiny interface showing lyrics input, score badge, and highlighted analysis](/images/projects/melodestiny-header.png)

## The Problem It Solves

There are plenty of lyric annotation sites (Genius, Musixmatch) and plenty of songwriting advice scattered across YouTube videos and masterclasses. What's missing is a tool that takes the actual craft knowledge embedded in those masterclasses — the structural rules, the rhythmic patterns, the vocabulary choices — and applies them algorithmically to your lyrics in seconds.

Songwriters get feedback from co-writers, producers, and A&R reps, but that feedback is subjective, slow, and expensive. Melodestiny doesn't replace that feedback. It gives you a structural X-ray of your lyrics before you take them to anyone else. Does the chorus arrive early enough? Is the hook memorable through repetition? Are the line lengths consistent enough to sing? Does the bridge introduce new vocabulary or just recycle the verses?

These are questions with measurable answers, and Melodestiny measures them.

## Features

### The 25 Techniques

Each technique produces a raw score from 0.0 to 1.0, weighted by importance, then combined into a final score out of 100. The techniques are grouped by the songwriters who popularized them:

| ID | Technique | Author | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| T01 | Melodic Math (Lyrical Rhythm) | Max Martin | 0.080 |
| T02 | Chorus-First Structural Check | Max Martin | 0.072 |
| T03 | Repetition Detection & Variation | Max Martin / Jack Antonoff | 0.064 |
| T04 | Mantra Density | Jack Antonoff | 0.048 |
| T05 | Hook/Title Structural Placement | Diane Warren | 0.080 |
| T06 | Singability Metrics | Max Martin | 0.056 |
| T07 | Line Length Consistency | Max Martin | 0.048 |
| T08 | Title Brevity | Diane Warren | 0.032 |
| T09 | Rhyme Scheme Consistency | Max Martin / Diane Warren | 0.064 |
| T10 | Section Length Parity | Max Martin | 0.040 |
| T11 | Vocabulary Simplicity | Diane Warren / Julia Michaels | 0.048 |
| T12 | Direct Address Pronoun Density | Diane Warren / Julia Michaels | 0.056 |
| T13 | Post-Chorus Hook Fragment | Pharrell Williams / Ed Sheeran | 0.040 |
| T14 | Bridge Vocabulary Novelty | Jack Antonoff / Max Martin | 0.032 |
| T15 | Structural Advisory | Max Martin / Jack Antonoff / Diane Warren | 0.040 |
| T16 | Emotional Arc Progression | Max Martin / Julia Michaels | 0.033 |
| T17 | Concrete Imagery Density | Julia Michaels / Diane Warren | 0.040 |
| T18 | Tense Consistency | Diane Warren | 0.027 |
| T19 | Section Contrast Score | Jack Antonoff | 0.033 |
| T20 | Conversational Flow | Pharrell Williams / Julia Michaels | 0.027 |
| T21 | First Line Hook | Ryan Tedder | 0.033 |
| T22 | Raw Emotion Words | Sia | 0.040 |
| T23 | Melodic Simplicity Score | Benny Blanco | 0.027 |
| T24 | Narrative Specificity | Ilsey Juber | 0.033 |
| T25 | Tension and Release | Linda Perry | 0.033 |

The final score maps to a label:

| Range | Label |
|---|---|
| 90–100 | Hit Potential |
| 75–89 | Strong Foundation |
| 60–74 | Good Bones |
| 45–59 | Work in Progress |
| 0–44 | Back to the Drawing Board |

### Highlighted Lyrics, Syllables, and Annotation Popovers

The lyrics display in **View Analysis** mode is highly interactive, transforming the plain text input into a rich typographical report:
- **Underlines and Annotations**: Every flagged line or phrase is underlined based on its significance:
  - **Gold underline** — Positive flag (technique applied well)
  - **Rust underline** — Warning flag (improvement opportunity)
  - **Blue-grey dashed underline** — Neutral note/tip
- **Multi-Annotation Support**: For text segments that trigger multiple songwriting techniques, highlights align cleanly. Tapping the segment opens a multi-annotation popover listing all matching tips, featuring a count badge (e.g., `3 Notes`) and custom scroll list grouping.
- **Syllable Division Markers & Counts**: Word syllables are split using the onset-principle and marked visually with subtle bullet separators (e.g., `Mel·o·des·ti·ny`). Each line displays its final syllable count (e.g., `8s`) at the right margin.
- **Line-Level Feedback Badges**: If a line contains feedback, a hoverable/tappable `(i)` badge appears next to the syllable count—colored gold for positive highlights or red for warnings—revealing specific suggestions instantly.

### Advanced Section Detection & Auto-Partitioning

Melodestiny automatically structures raw song lyrics into sections. It parses explicit square brackets (e.g., `[Chorus]`), colons (e.g., `Verse 1:`), and common songwriting keywords.
If no labels are present, a structural auto-partitioning algorithm segments the lyrics based on spacing patterns and repetition, labeling sections (e.g., `[Intro]`, `[Verse]`, `[Chorus]`, `[Outro]`) dynamically and flagging the UI with a notice recommending manual labels for optimal accuracy. Section titles are filtered out during calculation so they do not skew syllable or line-length averages.

### AI Lyric Generator (Best-of-Three)

A built-in AI lyric generation drawer integrates with OpenRouter. Users provide their own API key and choose from top models (like Claude 3.5 Sonnet or GPT-4o). The system uses a **Best-of-Three** workflow:
1. Generates three distinct variations in parallel.
2. Automatically passes all three candidates into the client-side Rust-compiled WebAssembly engine.
3. Compares the scores and injects the highest-scoring candidate directly into the editor for further refining.

### State Persistence, Reset, and Demo Hits

- **State Persistence**: Song titles and lyrics are automatically saved to `localStorage` as the user types, protecting creative progress across tab closures or accidental refreshes.
- **Application Reset**: A header-level reset button clears local storage and restores the clean, blank editor state immediately.
- **Demo Song Loader**: When the editor is blank, a prominent `✨ Load Demo Song` overlay lets users load one of 10 pre-loaded historical #1 pop hits (e.g., Max Martin-produced chart-toppers) with a single click to instantly test the tool's capabilities.

### Lyrical & Report Export

Users can download exports in two formats:
1. **Clean Raw Lyrics (TXT)**: Formatted copy of the text containing the latest lyric edits, removing all analysis highlights and syllable separator dots.
2. **Detailed Feedback Report (TXT/MD)**: A structured report containing the final wax-seal score, the 25-technique breakdown table, and a categorized list of high, medium, and low priority improvement opportunities complete with original line references.

## Architecture

The app is a single Next.js page with a two-column layout on desktop (input left, results right) and a stacked layout on mobile. The analysis engine is a Rust library compiled to WebAssembly that runs entirely in the browser.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Browser                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │          Next.js Frontend               │ │
│  │  (React, Tailwind, UI Components)       │ │
│  └──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘ │
│                 │                             │
│  ┌──────────────▼──────────────────────────┐ │
│  │        WASM Analysis Engine             │ │
│  │  (Rust compiled to WebAssembly)         │ │
│  │  - 25 algorithmic techniques            │ │
│  │  - Scoring & weighting                  │ │
│  │  - Lyric markup generation              │ │
│  │  - Syllable splitting & counting        │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### WASM Integration

The Rust crate exposes a single function to JavaScript:

```rust
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn analyze(title: &str, lyrics: &str) -> JsValue {
    // Returns serialized AnalysisOutput
}
```

The WASM module is loaded via dynamic import on app mount, cached as a singleton, and served from `/public/wasm/` with `Cache-Control: immutable` headers. The `.wasm` binary targets under 2MB gzipped, including the bundled 5000-word frequency list and the 2000-word CMU Pronouncing Dictionary subset.

### Key Algorithms

**Syllable counting and splitting** uses a multi-layered rule system. The vowel-group method handles base syllables, supplemented with special handling for vowel clusters, silent endings, and consonant splits:

```rust
fn count_syllables(word: &str) -> usize {
    let word = word.to_lowercase();
    let vowels: Vec<char> = "aeiouy".chars().collect();
    let mut count = 0;
    let mut prev_was_vowel = false;

    // 1. Count vowel groups
    for ch in word.chars() {
        let is_vowel = vowels.contains(&ch);
        if is_vowel && !prev_was_vowel {
            count += 1;
        }
        prev_was_vowel = is_vowel;
    }

    // 2. Adjust for magic-e pattern (consonant-vowel-consonant-e)
    if word.ends_with('e') && count > 1 {
        // Special case: check if it's not a "-le" ending preceded by a consonant
        let chars: Vec<char> = word.chars().collect();
        if chars.len() >= 2 && chars[chars.len() - 2] != 'l' {
            count -= 1;
        }
    }

    // 3. Adjust for silent -ed (e.g. "walked", but not "started")
    if word.ends_with("ed") && count > 1 {
        let chars: Vec<char> = word.chars().collect();
        if chars.len() >= 3 {
            let prev_char = chars[chars.len() - 3];
            if prev_char != 't' && prev_char != 'd' {
                count -= 1;
            }
        }
    }

    count.max(1)
}
```

For displaying visual dividers, the **syllable splitting** algorithm maps words by locating vowel nuclei, adjusting for silent trailing vowels, and applying the **onset principle** (consonants preceding a vowel group are grouped with that vowel's syllable, and multiple medial consonants split after the first consonant).

**Rhyme detection** operates in two tiers. It first queries the compiled CMU Pronouncing Dictionary subset for phonetic endings (stressed vowel and trailing phonemes, e.g., `"love"` -> `"AH V"`). If a word isn't in the subset, the engine falls back to heuristic matching: stripping common suffixes, checking vowel endings, and identifying common spelling rhyme pairs (e.g., `ee` and `ea`).

**Structural advisory (T15)** maps the song sections (e.g., `[Intro]`, `[Verse]`, `[Chorus]`, `[Bridge]`, `[Outro]`) to evaluate arrangement completeness. It checks for:
- Missing bridges (flagged if there are $\geq 2$ verses and $\geq 2$ choruses but no bridge).
- Missing pre-choruses (if line count ratio between verse and chorus exceeds $1.5$ or is below $0.67$).
- Structural errors (like back-to-back consecutive verses without chorus relief, or ending on a verse/bridge instead of the chorus payoff).

**Singability metrics** flag lines with dense consonant clusters (> 0.6 density) or very long syllables (> 4 characters per syllable) — the kinds of lines that are hard to sing a cappella walking down the street.

## Technology

The entire analysis pipeline runs client-side. No serverless functions, no API calls, no data sent anywhere. The frontend is Next.js with Tailwind CSS; the engine is Rust compiled to WebAssembly.

### Rust and WebAssembly

Rust was the right choice for the analysis engine for three reasons: performance (500 lines of lyrics analyzed in under 200ms), portability (the same crate compiles to WASM for the browser and can be wrapped in Tauri for native iOS/Android later), and correctness (the type system catches the kind of off-by-one errors that plague algorithmic text processing).

The WASM module bundles static data — a top-5000 English word frequency list derived from Google Books n-gram data and a 2000-word CMU Pronouncing Dictionary subset — so it's self-contained. No external lookups are required at runtime.

### Next.js and TypeScript

The frontend is a single-page Next.js app using the App Router. TypeScript is used throughout, with types defined once in Rust and translated to TypeScript interfaces. Tailwind CSS handles styling with a custom design token system for the antique aesthetic.

### Design Tokens

The visual system is built on muted earth tones and serif typography:

| Token | Value | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| `--color-parchment` | `#F5F0E8` | Main background |
| `--color-ink` | `#2C2418` | Primary text |
| `--color-sepia` | `#8B7355` | Secondary text |
| `--color-gold` | `#C4A265` | Scores, highlights, accents, syllable markers |
| `--color-rust` | `#A0522D` | Warnings, improvement flags |
| `--color-sage` | `#7A8B6F` | Positive indicators |
| `--color-blue-grey` | `#7B8FA1` | Note annotations |
| `--color-cream` | `#FBF8F1` | Card backgrounds |

Typography is **Mustopha** (a custom Arabic-inspired display font) for headings, Lora for body text, and IBM Plex Mono for scores and technical labels. Paper grain texture is applied via CSS noise. No sans-serif is used anywhere in the application.

## UI Design

The interface is a single-view, responsive layout. On mobile, input elements stack above results; on desktop, a two-column layout features independent scrolling, keeping the results sticky on the right. 

A custom `AnnotatedTextarea` component toggles the input field between **Edit Lyrics** (standard editing textarea) and **View Analysis** (interactive text displaying syllable markers, boundary divisions, hoverable feedback badges, and underlined annotations). The score badge is rendered as a wax-seal-style circular element that scales in with a spring overshoot animation. A 5×3 grid (desktop) or 3×5 grid (mobile) presents progress bars for all 25 technique scores. 

A client-side **Dark Mode** toggle switches the workspace to an organic, low-contrast slate-950 and gold theme, optimizing borders and text contrast for low-light studies.

## What I Learned

- **WASM bundle size is a real constraint.** Bundling a 2000-word phonetic dictionary and a 5000-word frequency list with the Rust crate means the WASM binary pushes toward the 2MB gzip target. Stripping debug symbols, using `wasm-opt`, and compressing the static data kept it under budget.
- **Client-side LLM coordination requires flexibility.** Integrating an AI Lyric Generator in the browser using OpenRouter requires prompting users for their own API keys and handling network requests gracefully. The **Best-of-Three** workflow runs parallel queries, meaning the UI needs to manage multiple loading states smoothly.
- **Section detection from plain text is harder than it sounds.** Users paste lyrics in wildly different formats — some label sections with brackets or colons, some don't, and some include timestamps or chord notation. The preprocessing pipeline filters out non-lyric data and maps coordinates, but the spacing-based auto-partitioning fallback is crucial for raw inputs.
- **Syllable splitting in English has massive edge cases.** Heuristics like the vowel-group method are approximate. Adjusting for magic-e, silent -ed endings, and consonant splits required layered exceptions in the Rust engine to avoid miscounts on common pop words (like "walked" or "moved").
- **Designing for an antique aesthetic without sacrificing usability is a balancing act.** Serif fonts, custom display typefaces like Mustopha, and textured backgrounds can tank readability if contrast ratios aren't managed. Every color combination, including the dark mode palette, was validated against WCAG AA thresholds.

## Challenges

- **Aligning overlays on editable text:** Developing the custom `AnnotatedTextarea` component required overlapping the text layers so highlights align exactly with the editable textarea's scroll position and font sizes.
- **Rhyme detection without a full phonetic dictionary:** The CMU Pronouncing Dictionary is too large (~134,000 entries) to bundle entirely. Combining a ~2000-word subset of common pop vocabulary with a heuristic suffix/diphthong fallback was necessary to stay within WebAssembly bundle limits.
- **Multi-annotation overlap management:** When a single line or word triggers multiple techniques (e.g. repetition and pronoun density), stacking highlights became messy. Merging overlaps into a unified popup with count badges and list layouts kept the UI clean.
- **Animation performance with many annotations:** A song with 50+ flagged lines means 50+ annotation underlines animating in sequence. Using CSS `@keyframes` with `animation-delay` rather than JavaScript-driven animations kept everything on the compositor thread.

## Future Work

- **Audio Input** — Upload audio, transcribe to lyrics via Whisper WASM, then analyze.
- **Rhyme Suggestions** — Suggest rhyming words for flagged lines using the phonetic dictionary.
- **Multi-language Support** — Extend the syllable counter and word frequency lists beyond English.
- **Collaboration** — Share analysis links and co-writing sessions.
- **Tauri Native App** — Wrap for iOS/Android using the shared Rust core.

## Links

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/ppsandwich/melodestiny)
- [Live App](https://melodestiny.vercel.app)

`,(0,n.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[(0,n.jsx)(e.strong,{children:"Syllable counting and splitting"})," uses a multi-layered rule system. The vowel-group method handles base syllables, supplemented with special handling for vowel clusters, silent endings, and consonant splits:"]}),`
`,(0,n.jsx)(e.pre,{children:(0,n.jsx)(e.code,{className:"hljs language-rust",children:`fn count_syllables(word: &str) -> usize {
    let word = word.to_lowercase();
    let vowels: Vec<char> = "aeiouy".chars().collect();
    let mut count = 0;
    let mut prev_was_vowel = false;

    // 1. Count vowel groups
    for ch in word.chars() {
        let is_vowel = vowels.contains(&ch);
        if is_vowel && !prev_was_vowel {
            count += 1;
        }
        prev_was_vowel = is_vowel;
    }

    // 2. Adjust for magic-e pattern (consonant-vowel-consonant-e)
    if word.ends_with('e') && count > 1 {
        // Special case: check if it's not a "-le" ending preceded by a consonant
        let chars: Vec<char> = word.chars().collect();
        if chars.len() >= 2 && chars[chars.len() - 2] != 'l' {
            count -= 1;
        }
    }

    // 3. Adjust for silent -ed (e.g. "walked", but not "started")
    if word.ends_with("ed") && count > 1 {
        let chars: Vec<char> = word.chars().collect();
        if chars.len() >= 3 {
            let prev_char = chars[chars.len() - 3];
            if prev_char != 't' && prev_char != 'd' {
                count -= 1;
            }
        }
    }

    count.max(1)
}
`})}),`

document.title: Sandwich Codes
document.description: An AI project portfolio and editorial website with a Rugrats-inspired postmodern aesthetic — handmade, asymmetrical, tactile, and expressive.

body.raw:
## Overview Sandwich Codes is an AI project portfolio and editorial website that doubles as its own showcase. It's the site you're looking at right now — a surreal 90s cartoon art department transformed into an experimental AI design studio. The aesthetic is deliberately off-kilter: Rugrats-era Nickelodeon meets editorial magazine meets sketchbook. Cards rotate slightly. Typography is oversized and asymmetrical. Doodles and scribbles float in the margins. Every surface has a paper-grain texture. The goal is to feel personal, strange, intelligent, and handmade — the opposite of a generic SaaS landing page. Underneath the visual chaos is a serious technical foundation. Content is authored in MDX and processed by Contentlayer at build time. Pages are statically generated with Next.js App Router. Animations use Framer Motion spring physics. The design system is built on Tailwind CSS with a custom token layer for colours, typography, shadows, and decorative components. ![Sandwich Codes homepage with hero section, featured projects, and hand-drawn decorative accents](/images/projects/sandwich-dot-codes-homepage.png) ## The Problem It Solves Most AI portfolio sites fall into one of two traps: either they're sterile, corporate-feeling dashboards with clean lines and muted palettes, or they're flashy-but-unreadable experiments that sacrifice usability for visual spectacle. Sandwich Codes occupies the middle ground — expressive and visually loud, but still readable, navigable, and fast. The site exists to showcase other projects (Expecto, Splinched, Sound of Wall) and to serve as a technical artifact in its own right. Every design decision — from the crooked card rotations to the hand-drawn SVG doodles — is implemented as a reusable component, not a one-off hack. ## Design System The design philosophy can be summarised as "intentional imperfection." Key principles: - **Asymmetry over symmetry** — Layouts deliberately avoid rigid centring. Elements rotate, offset, and overlap. - **Tactile surfaces** — Paper-grain textures, thick borders, layered shadows, and tape-strip effects make everything feel physical. - **Expressive typography** — Fraunces (a variable optical-size serif) handles headings with oversized, tightly-tracked weight. Inter handles body text. Line heights and spacing vary to create editorial rhythm. - **Dusty pastels** — The palette avoids pure white, pure black, and neon. Colours are warm and muted: mustard (`#D6B347`), teal (`#6F9D9A`), salmon (`#D98B73`), slime green (`#9FB06F`), lavender (`#B8A7CC`), and sky blue (`#9BB7D4`) sit on a cream background (`#F5EFE4`). - **Hand-drawn accents** — SVG doodles (stars, squiggles, circles, arrows, dot clusters) are scattered throughout as decorative elements at various scales, from 32px inline accents to 420px background watermarks. ### Decorative Component Library The site ships with a dedicated `/components/decorative` module containing reusable visual primitives: - **StickerTag** — Coloured label badges with slight rotation, used for tech stacks and status indicators. - **DoodleAccent** — Hand-drawn SVG shapes (star, circle, squiggle, arrow, x, dot-cluster) at configurable sizes and colours. - **TapeFrame** — A container that renders translucent tape strips at its corners, simulating photos taped to a surface. - **WobbleCard** — An interactive card that straightens and scales on hover with spring physics. - **PaperCard** — A static card variant with paper texture, thick borders, and tactile shadows. - **CrookedDivider** — Section dividers in scribble, wavy, and zigzag variants. - **NoiseOverlay** — A full-page subtle noise texture layer. - **ScribbleArrow** — A hand-drawn directional arrow. ## Content Architecture All content lives in the repository as MDX files, processed by Contentlayer at build time. Three content types are defined: - **Projects** (`/content/projects/`) — Full case studies with frontmatter for title, description, date, tags, status, cover image, tech stack, GitHub URL, and optional live demo link. - **Articles** (`/content/writing/`) — Long-form writing with reading time computed automatically. - **Experiments** (`/content/experiments/`) — Smaller playground projects and prototypes. Each content type has a corresponding schema in `contentlayer.config.ts` with required fields, computed fields (slug, reading time, URL), and MDX plugins for syntax highlighting, GitHub-flavoured markdown, and auto-linked headings. ## Technical Architecture ### Rendering Strategy The site uses Next.js App Router with a hybrid rendering approach: - **Static generation** for all content pages (projects, articles, experiments). Contentlayer generates typed objects at build time, and `generateStaticParams()` creates routes for each slug. - **Server components** by default. Client components are isolated to interactive elements: animations (`framer-motion`), search, theme toggle, and GitHub timeline polling. - **API routes** for GitHub data fetching (`/api/github-timeline`, `/api/github-languages`) with client-side polling every 90 seconds. ### GitHub Integration The homepage includes a live GitHub activity section that fetches public events (commits, pull requests, releases) from the GitHub REST API. A companion language chart aggregates bytes-per-language across all non-fork repositories. Data refreshes server-side every 60 seconds and client-side every 90 seconds. ### Styling Tailwind CSS handles all styling through a custom configuration that extends the default theme with: - Custom colour tokens mapped to the dusty-pastel palette. - `Fraunces` and `Inter` loaded via `next/font` with CSS variable injection. - Custom box-shadow utilities (`tactile`, `tactile-lg`, `tactile-sm`, `paper`) for the layered shadow effect. - A `.paper-grain` utility class and `.noise-overlay` for texture. - `.sticker`, `.tape-strip`, and `.crooked-border` component classes in the global CSS base layer. - Dark mode support via the `.dark` class with inverted backgrounds and preserved accent colours. ### Animation Framer Motion powers all motion on the site. The motion system follows strict rules: playful, springy, and imperfect. Allowed patterns include fade-up reveals with staggered delays, hover wobble effects, soft scaling, and gentle parallax. Corporate-smooth easing and sci-fi effects are explicitly avoided. ## Style Guide This section serves as the authoritative reference for code conventions across the Sandwich Codes codebase. Every rule here reflects what the code actually does, not aspirational goals. ### Formatting Enforced by Prettier (`.prettierrc`): | Rule | Value | |---|---| | Semicolons | Always | | Quotes | Double quotes | | Trailing commas | Always (objects, arrays, params) | | Tab width | 2 spaces | | Print width | 100 characters | | Line endings | LF | Run `npm run format` before committing. No ESLint config exists — rely on `next lint` defaults and Prettier for enforcement. ### Naming Conventions | Entity | Convention | Example | |---|---|---| | Component files | PascalCase | `ProjectCard.tsx`, `WobbleCard.tsx` | | Utility files | camelCase | `utils.ts`, `config.ts`, `github.ts` | | Component functions | PascalCase named export | `export function ProjectCard()` | | Props interfaces | PascalCase + `Props` suffix | `interface ProjectCardProps` | | Type definitions | PascalCase | `Project`, `NavItem`, `SiteConfig` | | Constants / config objects | camelCase | `siteConfig`, `statusColors` | | Variant maps | camelCase + `Styles` or `Colors` suffix | `variantStyles`, `statusColors` | | CSS custom classes | kebab-case | `.paper-grain`, `.noise-overlay`, `.tape-strip` | | CSS variables | kebab-case, double-dash prefix | `--font-fraunces`, `--font-inter` | | MDX content files | kebab-case | `sandwich-dot-codes.mdx`, `expecto.mdx` | | Route directories | kebab-case | `src/app/projects/[slug]/page.tsx` | ### Component Architecture #### Named Exports Only Every component uses a named function export. Default exports are not used anywhere in the codebase. ```tsx // Correct export function ProjectCard({ project, index = 0 }: ProjectCardProps) { return
...
; } // Incorrect — never do this export default function ProjectCard() { ... } ``` #### Props Interface Pattern Props are defined as an `interface` directly above the component. Components extend `React.HTMLAttributes` when they need to accept standard HTML attributes. ```tsx interface StickerTagProps extends React.HTMLAttributes { variant?: StickerVariant; rotation?: number; } export function StickerTag({ variant = "default", rotation = -2, className, children, ...props }: StickerTagProps) { // ... } ``` #### Variant Pattern Components that have visual variants use string literal union types and a `Record` map for styles: ```tsx type StickerVariant = "default" | "salmon" | "teal" | "mustard" | "slime" | "lavender" | "muted"; const variantStyles: Record = { default: "bg-background text-foreground", salmon: "bg-salmon text-foreground", // ... }; ``` #### Polymorphic `as` Prop Layout primitives accept an `as` prop for rendering as a different HTML element: ```tsx interface ContainerProps extends React.HTMLAttributes { as?: React.ElementType; size?: "default" | "narrow" | "wide"; } export function Container({ as: Component = "div", ... }: ContainerProps) { return {children}; } ``` ### "use client" Directive Only components that require client-side interactivity include the `"use client"` directive. This includes: - Components using `useState`, `useEffect`, or other React hooks - Components using Framer Motion (`motion`, `AnimatePresence`) - Components using browser APIs (`window`, `document`) - Components using Next.js client hooks (`useRouter`, `useSearchParams`, `usePathname`) Page components and data-fetching components remain as server components by default. Client interactivity is always delegated to the smallest possible leaf component. ### Class Name Merging All conditional class merging uses the `cn()` utility from `@/lib/utils`, which combines `clsx` and `tailwind-merge`: ```tsx import { cn } from "@/lib/utils";
``` Never use raw string concatenation or template literals for class composition. ### Imports Import order (enforced by convention, not a plugin): 1. React / Next.js imports 2. Third-party libraries (`framer-motion`, `lucide-react`, `date-fns`) 3. Contentlayer generated types (`contentlayer/generated`) 4. Internal components (`@/components/...`) 5. Internal utilities (`@/lib/...`) 6. Types / constants ```tsx import { useState } from "react"; import Link from "next/link"; import Image from "next/image"; import { motion } from "framer-motion"; import { ExternalLink, Github } from "lucide-react"; import type { Project } from "contentlayer/generated"; import { StickerTag } from "@/components/decorative/StickerTag"; import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"; ``` Always use the `@/` path alias. Never use relative paths like `../../lib/utils`. ### TypeScript - **Strict mode** is enabled. No `any` types. - **Contentlayer types** are imported with `import type` from `contentlayer/generated`. - **Custom types** live in `src/types/index.ts` and are exported as named types. - **Enum-like values** use string literal union types, not TypeScript `enum`: ```tsx status: "active" | "experimental" | "archived" ``` - **Optional fields** use `?` in the interface, never `| undefined`. ### Styling #### Tailwind-First All styling is done with Tailwind utility classes. Custom CSS is only used for effects that Tailwind cannot express (SVG textures, pseudo-element overlays, scroll styling). #### Class Order Follow this implicit ordering when writing Tailwind classes: 1. Layout (`flex`, `grid`, `block`, `relative`, `absolute`) 2. Sizing (`w-`, `h-`, `min-`, `max-`) 3. Spacing (`p-`, `m-`, `gap-`) 4. Typography (`font-`, `text-`, `leading-`, `tracking-`) 5. Visual (`bg-`, `border-`, `shadow-`, `opacity-`) 6. States (`hover:`, `focus:`, `dark:`) 7. Transitions (`transition-`, `duration-`) #### Design Tokens Always use the custom design tokens, never raw hex values in components: | Token | Tailwind Class | Hex | |---|---|---| | Background | `bg-background` / `bg-background-dark` | `#F5EFE4` / `#1a1816` | | Text | `text-foreground` / `text-foreground-dark` | `#292524` / `#e8e0d4` | | Accent | `text-mustard`, `bg-mustard` | `#D6B347` | | Link | `text-teal` | `#6F9D9A` | | Experiment | `text-salmon`, `bg-salmon` | `#D98B73` | | Active | `text-slime`, `bg-slime` | `#9FB06F` | | Decorative | `text-lavender`, `bg-lavender` | `#B8A7CC` | | Secondary text | `text-muted` | `#7C736C` | | Border | `border-border` | `#463F3A` | #### Border Width The site uses `border-3` (3px) as the standard border width, not the default `border` (1px). This is defined as a custom Tailwind utility. #### Box Shadows Four custom shadow utilities are available: | Utility | Usage | |---|---| | `shadow-tactile` | Standard card shadow (4px offset) | | `shadow-tactile-lg` | Hover/enhanced state (6px offset) | | `shadow-tactile-sm` | Small elements, tags (2px offset) | | `shadow-paper` | Subtle layered paper effect | #### Typography Scale Three custom fluid font sizes using `clamp()`: | Utility | Size Range | Usage | |---|---|---| | `text-display` | 3rem — 7rem | Hero headlines only | | `text-headline` | 2rem — 4.5rem | Section headings | | `text-subhead` | 1.5rem — 2.5rem | Subsection headings | Body text uses Tailwind defaults (`text-base`, `text-sm`, `text-lg`). #### Font Families | Utility | Font | Usage | |---|---|---| | `font-heading` | Fraunces (variable serif) | All headings, labels, tags | | `font-body` | Inter (sans-serif) | Body text, descriptions, UI | Both are loaded via `next/font/google` and injected as CSS variables (`--font-fraunces`, `--font-inter`) in the root layout. ### Animation All animation uses Framer Motion. The rules: - **Spring physics only.** No CSS easing, no `tween`. Use `{ type: "spring", stiffness: 200, damping: 20 }`. - **Fade-up reveals.** Initial state: `opacity: 0, y: 30`. Animate to: `opacity: 1, y: 0`. - **Staggered delays.** Use `delay: index * 0.1` for list items. - **Scroll-triggered.** Use `whileInView` with `viewport={{ once: true }}`. - **Hover effects.** Use `whileHover` and `whileTap` on interactive elements. - **No sci-fi effects.** No glows, no particle systems, no 3D transforms. The motion language is playful and springy, not sleek and corporate. - **Respect reduced motion.** The global CSS handles this via `prefers-reduced-motion`. Components should not duplicate this logic. ### Decorative Components Decorative elements follow strict accessibility rules: - Always include `aria-hidden="true"` on decorative elements. - Always include `pointer-events-none` so they don't interfere with interaction. - Use responsive visibility (`hidden sm:block`, `hidden lg:block`) to hide on small screens where they might obscure content. - Decorative components live in `src/components/decorative/` and are exported from the barrel `index.ts`. ### Page Pattern Every page follows this structure: ```tsx // 1. Import metadata type (for static metadata) or generateMetadata function import type { Metadata } from "next"; // 2. Import Contentlayer types import { allProjects } from "contentlayer/generated"; // 3. Import sorting/comparison utilities import { compareDesc } from "date-fns"; // 4. Import client component for interactive parts import { ProjectsPageContent } from "@/components/projects/ProjectsPageContent"; // 5. Static metadata export export const metadata: Metadata = { title: "Projects", description: "...", }; // 6. Default function — server component, data fetching at top export default function ProjectsPage() { const projects = allProjects.sort((a, b) => compareDesc(new Date(a.date), new Date(b.date)), ); return ; } ``` Dynamic routes use `generateStaticParams()` for static generation and `generateMetadata()` for per-page SEO. ### API Route Pattern API routes live in `src/app/api/[name]/route.ts`: - Export named HTTP method handlers: `export async function GET()`, `export async function POST()`. - Return `NextResponse.json()` with explicit `Cache-Control` headers. - Export `revalidate` for ISR caching where applicable. - Use Zod for request body validation. - Always return proper error status codes with JSON error bodies. ### Barrel Exports Each component directory has an `index.ts` barrel file that re-exports all components: ```tsx export { StickerTag } from "./StickerTag"; export { DoodleAccent } from "./DoodleAccent"; export { PaperCard } from "./PaperCard"; // ... ``` Import from the barrel, not individual files: ```tsx import { StickerTag, DoodleAccent } from "@/components/decorative"; ``` ### MDX Content #### Frontmatter All required fields must be present. Dates use ISO format (`YYYY-MM-DD`). Tags are lowercase strings. Status values are one of `active`, `experimental`, or `archived`. #### Images Use standard Markdown image syntax with alt text: ```mdx ![Alt text describing the image](/images/projects/example.png) ``` #### Headings Start at `##` (h2). The page title comes from frontmatter and renders as h1. Use `###` for subsections within h2 sections. ### Dark Mode The site uses the `class` strategy for dark mode (`next-themes`). Dark mode colours are defined as `.dark` variants in Tailwind config and in global CSS. When adding new colours or components: - Use Tailwind's `dark:` prefix for dark-mode overrides. - The `bg-background` / `bg-background-dark` tokens handle page backgrounds. - Accent colours (mustard, teal, salmon, etc.) remain the same in both modes. - Text and background colours invert; borders shift to lighter values. ## Development Process The project was built in phases: 1. **Foundation** — Next.js initialisation, Tailwind configuration with the custom token layer, typography setup (Fraunces + Inter via `next/font`), and layout primitives (Container, Section, Navbar, Footer). 2. **Content layer** — Contentlayer configuration with Project, Article, and Experiment schemas. MDX plugins for syntax highlighting, slug generation, and auto-linked headings. Dynamic route generation with `generateStaticParams()`. 3. **Pages** — Homepage with hero, featured projects grid, writing preview, and GitHub activity sections. Individual project and article pages with editorial layouts. 4. **Design system** — Decorative component library (StickerTag, DoodleAccent, TapeFrame, WobbleCard, PaperCard, CrookedDivider, NoiseOverlay, ScribbleArrow). Noise texture overlay. Paper-grain CSS utility. 5. **Interactivity** — Framer Motion animation system. GitHub API integration with polling. Project filtering by status. Responsive design pass. ## Challenges ### Balancing Chaos and Usability The biggest challenge was maintaining readability while making everything look intentionally imperfect. Crooked cards need to still be clickable. Oversized typography needs to still flow on mobile. Doodles need to not obscure content. Every decorative element uses `pointer-events-none`, `aria-hidden="true"`, and responsive visibility classes (`hidden sm:block`, `hidden lg:block`) to ensure they enhance rather than interfere. ### Static Content with Live Data The site mixes statically-generated MDX content with live GitHub API data. Contentlayer handles the static side at build time, while API routes and client-side polling handle the dynamic side. The GitHub section uses `useEffect` with `setInterval` for polling, `useState` for optimistic updates, and graceful fallbacks when the API is unavailable or rate-limited. ### SVG Favicon The site uses an SVG favicon (`src/app/icon.svg`) — a hand-drawn sandwich in the site's colour palette (mustard bread, green lettuce, salmon filling). Next.js App Router automatically serves this as the favicon without additional configuration. ## Future Improvements - **Search** — Full-text search across projects and articles using Typesense, with fuzzy matching and tag filtering. - **Command palette** — A `cmdk`-powered command palette for keyboard-driven navigation. - **Embedded demos** — Inline iframe embeds of live project demos directly within project pages. - **RSS feed** — An RSS feed for the writing section. - **Analytics** — Privacy-friendly analytics via Plausible. - **OG image generation** — Dynamic OpenGraph images per project using `@vercel/og`. ## Links - [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/ppsandwich/sandwich-dot-codes) - [Live Site](https://sandwich-dot-codes.vercel.app)

document.title: Sound of Wall
document.description: Upload audio. The app analyzes it in-browser, extracts sonic features, and generates deterministic procedural artwork — the same audio always produces the same image.

body.raw:
## Overview

Sound of Wall turns uploaded audio into collectible procedural artwork. You drop in a sound file — a song, a field recording, a voice memo — and the app analyzes it entirely in the browser, extracting rhythm, spectral content, dynamics, and structural features. Those features drive a deterministic rendering engine that produces unique visual compositions from the audio's sonic fingerprint.

The critical constraint: the same audio input always generates the same artwork. No randomness, no AI inference, no diffusion models. Every pixel is derived from mathematical relationships between the audio's properties and a seeded procedural system. Change the audio, and the output changes. Keep the audio identical, and the output is identical — every time, on every device.

## The Problem It Solves

Generative art tools either require deep technical knowledge (Processing, TouchDesigner) or lean on AI image generation (Midjourney, Stable Diffusion). The first approach excludes most people. The second produces non-deterministic, non-reproducible output that can't be meaningfully tied to its input.

Sound of Wall occupies a different space. It's a direct audio-to-visual pipeline where the mapping is transparent and deterministic. The artwork isn't an interpretation or an approximation — it's a mathematical transformation. The same audio file produces the same composition whether you run it today or a year from now.

This makes the output collectible in a way AI-generated images aren't. The artwork is a function of the sound, not a function of a prompt and a random seed.

## Development Process

The project required building four tightly coupled systems: audio analysis, deterministic hashing, scene generation, and procedural rendering. Each system feeds the next, and the entire pipeline runs client-side.

### Browser Audio Analysis

Audio analysis happens entirely in the browser using the Web Audio API and Meyda. The pipeline:

1. **Decode** — The uploaded file is decoded into an `AudioBuffer` via `AudioContext.decodeAudioData()`.
2. **Normalize** — The waveform data is normalized to remove volume variance between files.
3. **FFT Analysis** — The audio is broken into overlapping windows and analyzed via FFT.
4. **Feature Extraction** — Meyda extracts a feature vector from each window.
5. **Aggregate** — Window-level features are aggregated into a single feature vector for the entire file.

Extracted features include:

- **Rhythm**: BPM, beat density, transient sharpness, rhythmic complexity
- **Spectral**: spectral centroid, brightness, warmth, noisiness, harmonic density
- **Dynamic**: loudness, dynamic range, energy evolution over time
- **Structural**: repetition patterns, transition density, tension curves

The feature vector is the bridge between raw audio and visual output. Every rendering decision downstream derives from it.

### Deterministic Hashing

Once the feature vector is computed, a SHA-256 hash is generated from the normalized waveform data and feature vector using the SubtleCrypto API. This hash becomes the deterministic seed for all downstream generation.

The hash explicitly ignores:

- Filenames
- Metadata (ID3 tags, etc.)
- Album art
- Timestamps

The hash explicitly includes:

- Normalized waveform data
- Extracted feature vectors

This ensures that two different files with identical audio content produce identical artwork, while two files with different audio content — regardless of filename — produce different artwork.

### Seeded PRNG

All randomness in the rendering pipeline uses a Mulberry32 seeded PRNG initialized from the SHA-256 hash. `Math.random()` is never used in any rendering logic. This guarantees that the same seed produces the same sequence of random values, which means the same scene definition, the same geometry, the same colors, and the same final image.

### Scene Generation

The feature vector and seed drive a scene definition generator that produces a JSON structure describing every visual parameter:

```json
{
  "seed": 92837123,
  "palette": ["#1e1033", "#5e239d", "#f61067"],
  "geometryDensity": 0.72,
  "noiseScale": 0.41,
  "symmetry": 3,
  "particleCount": 1200,
  "energyCurve": [0.2, 0.7, 0.3, 0.9],
  "stylePreset": "ethereal"
}
```

Scene definitions are stored in a database so they can be retrieved, re-rendered, and shared without re-analyzing the audio.

### Procedural Rendering Engine

The rendering engine uses the Canvas API to draw deterministic procedural compositions. All geometry, noise fields, color systems, and composition rules are derived from the scene definition — no randomness enters at render time.

Visual systems include:

- **Geometry**: spline systems, recursive curves, radial compositions, particle fields, Voronoi patterns
- **Texture**: Perlin noise, simplex noise, turbulence fields, grain overlays, dithering
- **Composition**: derived from rhythmic density, harmonic complexity, dynamic range, and energy progression

Heavy rendering runs in a Web Worker using OffscreenCanvas to avoid blocking the React UI thread. Standard renders complete in under 5 seconds. High-resolution 4096×4096 PNG exports target under 15 seconds.

### Style Presets

Six style presets modify palettes, symmetry, geometry density, texture intensity, and noise behavior:

- **Ethereal** — soft gradients, low density, high symmetry
- **Noir** — high contrast, monochromatic, sharp geometry
- **Brutalist** — raw geometry, heavy grain, aggressive composition
- **Psychedelic** — saturated palettes, high noise scale, recursive patterns
- **Minimal** — sparse composition, muted palettes, clean lines
- **Retro Futurist** — CRT textures, scanlines, neon palettes

The same audio with different presets produces visually distinct but deterministically reproducible results.

## Technology

### Frontend

Next.js App Router with React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion. Hosted on Vercel.

### Serverless Backend

Vercel Route Handlers handle orchestration only — no heavy computation runs on the server. Serverless functions target sub-second duration.

### Audio Analysis

Web Audio API for decoding and playback. Meyda for feature extraction. All analysis runs in the browser.

### Rendering

Canvas API with OffscreenCanvas in Web Workers. simplex-noise for noise fields. The entire rendering pipeline is client-side.

### Database

Neon Postgres stores generation metadata: audio hash, seed, style preset, feature vector, scene definition, image URL, and timestamp. If an identical audio hash already exists, the system returns the existing scene definition and image without regenerating.

### Storage

Vercel Blob stores uploaded audio, exported images, and thumbnails.

## UI Design

The interface is dark, cinematic, and gallery-like. The upload experience supports drag-and-drop and click-to-upload with progress indication and an audio preview player. Supported formats: MP3, WAV, FLAC, OGG, M4A. Max size: 100MB. Max duration: 15 minutes.

The generation page shows the audio analysis in real-time — waveform, feature extraction progress, and the rendering canvas. Once complete, the user can export at up to 4096×4096 PNG, switch style presets to see different deterministic outputs from the same audio, and share the artwork via a permanent URL.

The gallery page displays generated artworks in a responsive grid with hover animations and mixed style presets. Individual artwork pages show the full scene definition, the audio that generated it, and sharing controls.

Mobile devices gracefully reduce render resolution and particle count to maintain performance.

## What I Learned

- Deterministic generation is harder than it sounds. Floating-point differences across browsers mean you need to normalize aggressively and avoid relying on platform-specific math precision.
- The Web Audio API's `decodeAudioData` behaves differently across browsers for edge cases (corrupted headers, unusual sample rates). Defensive normalization is essential.
- OffscreenCanvas support is still inconsistent. Safari on iOS doesn't support it, so the rendering engine needs a fallback path that renders on the main thread with requestAnimationFrame chunking to avoid blocking.
- SHA-256 via SubtleCrypto is fast but asynchronous. The hashing pipeline needs to be designed around Promises from the start.
- Storing scene definitions as JSONB in Postgres is convenient but requires careful indexing on the audio hash column for the deduplication cache lookup to be fast.

## Challenges

- **Cross-browser determinism**: Ensuring the same audio produces pixel-identical output across Chrome, Safari, Firefox, and Edge required abstracting all math operations and avoiding browser-specific FFT implementations.
- **High-resolution export**: Rendering a 4096×4096 canvas in the browser without crashing required tiling the render into chunks and compositing them via OffscreenCanvas.
- **Feature vector quality**: Simple audio features (RMS, zero-crossing rate) don't capture enough information to drive visually interesting compositions. The feature extraction pipeline needed spectral centroid, MFCCs, chroma, and energy evolution curves to produce compelling output.
- **Caching identical uploads**: The deduplication system needs to handle the case where two users upload the same audio — same content, different filenames, different metadata. The hash must be content-addressed, not metadata-addressed.

document.title: Splinched
document.description: A web-based sample chopper and sequencer that uses multiple algorithms to automatically slice audio, then rearranges the slices into new patterns using 128+ built-in sequences.

body.raw:
## Overview

Splinched is a web-based sample chopping and sequencing tool built for music producers. You upload or record a piece of audio, and the app automatically slices it using a variety of algorithms — transient detection, beat-synced, random, even-spaced, and more. Each algorithm produces a different set of slices, and every variant can be previewed independently.

The slices are then loaded into a step sequencer with 128+ built-in patterns — half rhythmic, half melodic — each with 16, 32, or 64 steps. The output can be previewed in the browser and downloaded as 16-bit WAV files, individually or bundled in a ZIP.

The core idea is that chopping samples shouldn't require music theory knowledge or a pre-existing vision. Splinched embraces randomness and happy accidents. You feed it audio, it gives you back something you wouldn't have thought to make.

![Splinched header with logo and tagline](/images/projects/splinched-header.png)

## The Problem It Solves

Traditional sample chopping in DAWs like Ableton or on hardware like the SP-404 requires the producer to already know what they want the output to sound like. You need to understand transients, bar structure, and rhythmic phrasing to make meaningful chops. Tools like XLN Audio's Life plugin enable a more random workflow, but they're designed for drum loops and have limited sequencer pattern diversity.

Splinched removes that barrier. It applies every supported chopping algorithm to your audio simultaneously, so you can audition dozens of slice variations without manual work. The sequencer patterns range from straightforward drum patterns to complex melodic sequences that re-pitch slices according to a detected or user-specified key and mode.

## Development Process

The project started with the audio engine. The Web Audio API's `AudioContext` and `OfflineAudioContext` handle all playback and rendering, but the real complexity lives in the slicing algorithms.

### Chopping Algorithms

The app supports at least 6 chopping methods, each taking a different approach to dividing audio:

- **Transients** — Detects amplitude peaks in the waveform to find natural attack points. This is the default algorithm and works well for most musical material.
- **Beat-Synced** — Divides the audio based on the detected or user-set BPM, slicing at musically relevant grid positions.
- **Random** — Places slice boundaries at random positions, weighted toward areas of higher amplitude.
- **Even** — Divides the audio into equal-length segments regardless of content.
- **Frequency-Based** — Uses spectral analysis to find points where the frequency content changes significantly.
- **Onset Detection** — A hybrid approach that combines amplitude and spectral flux to detect note onsets.

Each algorithm runs independently, and the user can switch between them to compare outputs. The slice count is configurable: 2, 4, 6, 8, 16, 32, 64, or 256 slices, with 16 as the default.

### Sequencer Patterns

The sequencer is the heart of the app. It has 128+ patterns split evenly between rhythmic and melodic categories:

- **Rhythmic patterns** are weighted toward slices identified as drums. They include standard drum patterns, syncopated grooves, and polyrhythmic sequences. Some include micro-timing offsets (random ±0–30ms shifts) and ratchets (rapid repeated notes) for humanization.
- **Melodic patterns** limit the pitch of slices according to a user-selectable key, mode (major, minor, dorian, etc.), and octave. Slices identified as drums are automatically excluded from melodic patterns. Melodic patterns play slices chromatically within the constraints of the selected scale.

![Drum sequencer pattern with rhythmic slice assignments](/images/projects/splinched-sequencer-drum.png)

![Melodic sequencer pattern with pitch-mapped slices](/images/projects/splinched-sequencer-melody.png)

Patterns can be 16, 32, or 64 steps. Steps wrap to new lines in the display. A live playhead tracks playback across the waveform preview and the sequencer grid simultaneously.

### Pattern Manipulation

![Sequencer settings with density, syncopation, and swing controls](/images/projects/splinched-sequencer-settings.png)

Beyond selecting a pattern, users have deep control over how it plays:

- **Density slider** — Adjusts the ratio of active to inactive steps. Acts as a non-destructive mask that preserves manual edits underneath.
- **Syncopation slider** — Controls the number of repeated slices in the pattern. Higher values = more repetition.
- **Swing control** — Shifts timing of off-beat steps for groove adjustment.
- **Euclidean generator** — Generates Euclidean rhythms on the fly with visible Steps and Pulses controls.
- **Re-roll button** — Randomizes which slices are assigned to which active steps in the sequencer.
- **Note Repeat** — Percentage likelihood of a step having a ratcheted/repeated note (default 0%).
- **Micro-Timing** — Percentage likelihood of a step being offset by a random 0–30ms amount (default 0%).
- **Probability** — Percentage likelihood that each step is triggered at all (default 100%).

### ADSR Envelope

A user-controlled ADSR (Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release) envelope shapes every sample played through the sequencer. The sliders are logarithmic, with a range of 0–1000ms. Default values: A 20ms, D 150ms, S 0ms, R 80ms. The envelope is visualized as a curve where each section's length reflects its current value, and the total duration is displayed inside the visualization.

An "Allow Deviation" toggle introduces per-slice randomness: when active, each slice's envelope deviates by ±50% from the set values.

### Audio Analysis

Before the sequencer can make intelligent decisions, each slice needs to be analyzed:

- **Pitch detection** — Each slice's pitch is analyzed and displayed on the sequencer steps and waveform preview.
- **Instrument classification** — Slices are categorized by instrument type (drums, tonal, etc.), which determines how they're weighted in rhythmic vs. melodic patterns.
- **Key and mode detection** — The uploaded audio's musical key and mode are auto-detected and displayed (e.g., "Auto-detected: D Minor"). The user can override this.

### Filtering

Three-band EQ controls (Low, Mid, High) as vertical sliders, defaulting to 50%. Values above 50% boost, below 50% cut. This shapes the tonal character of the output before export.

## Technology

The entire app runs client-side in the browser. No server, no backend, no external dependencies at runtime.

### Web Audio API

All audio processing — playback, slicing, envelope shaping, filtering, and rendering for export — uses the Web Audio API. `AudioContext` handles real-time playback, while `OfflineAudioContext` renders exports at the correct sample rate without blocking the UI.

### Web MIDI API

MIDI connectivity is initialized on demand via a button press. Once connected, a MIDI keyboard can trigger individual slices chromatically. The app maps MIDI note numbers to slice indices with pitch transposition.

![MIDI pads interface for triggering slices](/images/projects/splinched-midi-pads.png)

### Canvas API

The waveform display is rendered on a `<canvas>` element. Slice boundaries are drawn as vertical lines over the waveform. When a slice is triggered by the sequencer, it gets a visual highlight that persists for the duration of the slice's playback. Clicking a slice in the waveform toggles its corresponding steps in the sequencer.

Slices that are used in active sequencer steps are highlighted in the waveform preview at all times, giving a visual map of which parts of the audio are actually being used.

### Drag and Drop

The app supports a full drag-and-drop workflow:
- Drag a slice from the waveform onto a sequencer step
- Drag a slice from the waveform onto a pad in the slice bank
- Drag a slice from the slice bank onto a sequencer step
- Drag a slice from a sequencer step back onto a pad

### Persistence

All settings, uploaded audio, and generated patterns persist in `localStorage`. Refreshing the page restores the full state — the audio, the current pattern, all slider positions, and any manually edited steps. Users can save named sequences to localStorage and recall them from a dropdown.

### Export

Output is rendered as 16-bit WAV files using a custom WAV encoder. Users can download individual samples or bundle everything into a single ZIP file using JSZip. The export includes a configurable loop count: the sequence rendered 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 times.

### Keyboard Shortcuts

Every control has a visible keyboard shortcut. All shortcuts are displayed on-screen. The app is fully keyboard-navigable and A11y compliant.

## UI Design

![Configuration panel with algorithm selection and slice count options](/images/projects/splinched-config.png)

The interface is designed to look and feel like professional music software — dense with controls, but organized. All controls are accessible within a single click, not buried in menus or context actions.

Each UI section (waveform, sequencer, ADSR, filters, patterns) can be collapsed and expanded by clicking its heading. Collapsed sections are visually distinct so it's clear they can be expanded.

Sliders show visual feedback: when at a default value, they're greyed out. When deviating from default, a line shows the direction and magnitude of the deviation. All sliders have a small snap zone around their default value.

The sequencer steps wrap to new lines as needed. Each step shows its slice number and, for melodic patterns, its musical note. Steps can be toggled by clicking or by clicking and dragging across multiple steps. Micro-timing and ratchets are visually indicated on the steps where they apply.

The UI is optimized for mobile — all controls remain accessible on a smartphone screen.

## What I Learned

- The Web Audio API is incredibly capable but has sharp edges around timing. `AudioContext.currentTime` is your source of truth, and scheduling ahead with `noteOn`/`noteOff` is essential for tight playback.
- Pitch detection in the browser is hard. Simple autocorrelation works for monophonic material but breaks down on complex mixes. For polyphonic audio, you need spectral analysis.
- `localStorage` has a ~5MB limit. Storing raw audio data requires encoding it efficiently (base64-encoded WAV) and being mindful of quota.
- Building a sequencer UI that works equally well with mouse, touch, and MIDI requires careful event handling. Pointer events abstract over all three input methods.

## Challenges

- **Latency**: Getting slice playback to feel immediate required pre-loading all slice buffers into memory and scheduling playback slightly ahead of the audio clock.
- **ADSR visualization**: Rendering the logarithmic envelope curve in real-time as sliders move required a custom canvas renderer that maps logarithmic values to screen coordinates.
- **Pattern storage**: 128+ patterns with variable step counts, note assignments, micro-timing, and ratchets is a lot of data. Compressing the pattern format while keeping it human-readable was a design challenge.
- **Drag and drop across contexts**: Dragging a slice from the waveform (canvas) to a sequencer step (DOM) requires careful coordinate mapping and custom drag state management.

`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"The core idea is that chopping samples shouldn't require music theory knowledge or a pre-existing vision. Splinched embraces randomness and happy accidents. You feed it audio, it gives you back something you wouldn't have thought to make."}),`

`,(0,n.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Splinched removes that barrier. document.title: Frontend Design
document.purpose: Create premium, high-end frontend interfaces with an Australian organic design sensibility: flowing shapes, mature typography, tactile materials, restrained colour, handcrafted details, and polished art direction.

body.raw:
> [!NOTE]
> View the original raw Markdown rules document [here](https://github.com/ppsandwich/sandwich-dot-codes/blob/main/content/skills/FRONTEND_DESIGN_PPS.md) on GitHub.

## The Skill

This skill defines a premium, handcrafted approach to frontend design, characterized by an "Australian Organic" aesthetic. It emphasizes interfaces that feel art-directed, distinctive, and useful, avoiding generic AI or SaaS boilerplate designs. 

### 1. Core Philosophy & Quality Bar
* Design with a clear visual direction: confident, composed, and tailored to the product's purpose.
* Interfaces must look expensive and polished, similar to work from a high-end independent design studio.
* **References:** Align the aesthetic with projects like Sandwich Codes (creative, odd, indie), Melodestiny (whimsical, music-adjacent), and Knut Counter (practical, simple, quietly playful).

### 2. Australian Audience Principles
* **Light:** Bright, dry, sun-bleached, and open.
* **Materials:** Tactile textures like paper, canvas, painted timber, terrazzo, ceramic, and matte surfaces.
* **Colour:** Grounded, natural palettes (eucalyptus, sage, clay, ochre, sand, bone, charcoal).
* **Space:** Relaxed, generous, and breathable.
* **Tone:** Direct, plainspoken, useful, and slightly playful. Avoid corporate buzzwords and motivational sludge.

### 3. Expensive UI Rules
* **Spacing:** Use generous, intentional whitespace. Avoid cramped forms and dense cards.
* **Typography:** Rely on strong typography as the primary design material. Use distinctive display faces paired with readable body fonts.
* **Fewer, Better Ideas:** Focus on one or two strong visual ideas rather than heavy decoration.
* **Materiality & Imperfection:** Incorporate subtle physical cues (paper grain, soft shadows) and controlled imperfection (organic curves, off-grid accents).
* **Colour Restraint:** Use a restrained palette with one memorable accent.
* **Custom Components:** Ensure buttons, cards, forms, and navigation feel carefully designed and tactile.

### 4. Layout, Shape & Motion
* **Shape Language:** Employ organic, flowing shapes (uneven pills, blob panels, soft warping) instead of sterile, rigid rectangles.
* **Layout Principles:** Create composed layouts with soft asymmetry, offset cards, and editorial-style sections. Clarity must still take precedence.
* **Motion:** Use gentle, physical, and useful animations (soft hover lift, small reveals, smooth easing). Avoid bouncy, excessive, or blocking motion.

---

## Challenges

- **Balancing Restraint and Personality**: Ensuring the design feels characterful and handcrafted without becoming cluttered, overly decorative, or losing usability.
- **Maintaining Accessibility**: Utilizing a muted, organic color palette with low-contrast aesthetics while ensuring text contrast and interactive states remain accessible.
- **Avoiding Generic Patterns**: Overcoming the default "AI-generated" or typical SaaS look by implementing custom, slightly unpredictable layouts without relying on standard boilerplate libraries.

---

## Future Improvements

- **Component Library**: Developing a reusable set of React UI components (like buttons, organic cards, and form inputs) that inherently embody the Australian organic aesthetic for faster prototyping.
- **Micro-Interaction System**: Documenting and standardizing the subtle motion curves and physical-feeling interactions to ensure consistency across different products.
- **Automated Contrast Checking**: Integrating design tools to automatically verify that the sun-washed, tactile colour palettes meet WCAG accessibility standards.

document.title: Project Invention
document.purpose: A methodology for generating buildable, creative project concepts tailored to my hobbies, PM background, and portfolio aesthetics.

body.raw:
> [!NOTE]
> View the original raw Markdown rules document [here](https://github.com/ppsandwich/sandwich-dot-codes/blob/main/content/skills/SKILL_INVENTION.md) on GitHub.

## The Skill

This skill outlines a systematic methodology for inventing project, app, and portfolio concepts that fit my unique profile: a creative, product-minded individual based in Melbourne with a variety of hobbies and a diverse career background. The goal is to generate ideas that are distinct, buildable, and high in portfolio value, avoiding generic SaaS or startup boilerplate.

### 1. Core Invention Principles
* **Personal Relevance & Buildability:** Combine my interests (LEGO, music, gaming, coding, DJing, art, poetry) with realistic solo build constraints.
* **Specific Tools over Vague Platforms:** Prioritize tight, functional utilities (e.g., setlist energy mapper) rather than large, complex networks or marketplaces.
* **Obvious Demo Moment:** Ensure each idea has a clear "show me" user workflow (e.g., input file/text -> receive visual or audio output) demoable in under 60 seconds.
* **Small, Complete, Polished:** Focus on a single excellent workflow, strong visual identity, and minimal backend (Vercel serverless friendly, no accounts required).
* **Deliberate AI Use:** Choose the build mode (No AI, Optional AI, AI-assisted, or AI-core) based on whether it is genuinely justified.
* **Privacy & Safety First:** Prefer local-first client-side data processing. Avoid storing sensitive identity data or building surveillance-like tools.
* **Authentic Voice:** Present ideas in a dry, practical, observant, and slightly odd tone, strictly avoiding corporate hype words.

### 2. Intersection Formula
Generate unique project ideas by combining:
* **One Interest:** e.g., LEGO, DJing, Poetry, Writing, Photography.
* **One Work Context:** Retail, Advertising, Insurance, Ecommerce, Accounting.
* **One Playful Mechanic:** Draggable blocks, game loops, visual fossils.
* **One Build Constraint:** Seeded client-side generator, Web Audio API, Canvas.

### 3. Preferred Output Patterns
* **Creative Input → Structured Output:** e.g., lyrics to melody, DJ setlist to energy curve, photo to colour palette.
* **Data → Playful Object:** Turning API usage, weather, or plans into physical/visual metaphors.
* **Constraint-Based Generators:** Restricting options (e.g., 5-note song generator) to trigger creativity.
* **Strange Interfaces for Practical Tools:** Prioritizing tools (e.g., product priority game) with memorable, custom interactions.

---

## Challenges

- **Avoiding Cliché & Hype**: Ensuring concepts bypass generic B2B SaaS tropes and remain aligned with my practical, product-minded, yet slightly quirky design taste.
- **Scope Calibration**: Designing highly compelling and visual demos while maintaining a strictly scoped MVP that a single developer or AI coding agent can build in a few days.
- **Coherent Intersections**: Crafting natural intersections between very different domains (e.g., LEGO and cryptography, or retail and game mechanics) without producing overly complex or confusing user flows.

---

## Future Improvements

- **Interactive Idea Generator**: Developing a mini web-app version of this skill that dynamically rolls random intersections (Interest + Domain + Constraint) to jumpstart brainstorming.
- **Project Starter Boilerplates**: Building ready-to-use Next.js + Tailwind + Canvas/WebAudio templates to reduce the setup time for new projects.
- **Portfolio Health Dashboard**: Creating a visualization tool for the homepage to track portfolio coverage of different interests and highlight gaps where new ideas are needed.

document.title: Personality & Tone
document.purpose: Act as a style and personality guide for writing, drafting, and roleplaying in my distinct voice, based on a private chat export and my known context.

body.raw:
> [!NOTE]
> View the original raw Markdown rules document [here](https://github.com/ppsandwich/sandwich-dot-codes/blob/main/content/skills/SKILL_PERSONALITY_PPS.md) on GitHub.

## The Skill

This skill defines my core personality, tone, humour, and conversational style. It provides instructions on how to write in my voice for different contexts, ranging from casual private chats to more structured public writing.

### 1. Core Personality
* **Traits:** Curious, dry, practical, self-aware, slightly chaotic, product-minded, and detail-oriented.
* **Interests:** Music production, coding, LEGO, 3D printing, smart home tech, weird internet products, pets (Bun), and finding good deals.
* **Dislikes:** Bad design, useless included screws, overpriced tech, corporate sludge, and systems that make no sense.

### 2. Conversational Style
* **Private Chat:** Typo-prone, lowercase-heavy, punctuation-light. Thoughts unfold across multiple short messages ("yeah true", "tbh", "i think", "also").
* **Humour:** Dry, self-aware, and slightly dark. Uses precise observation, deadpan exaggeration, and slightly cursed metaphors.
* **Emotional Register:** Expresses care through practical advice, problem-solving, and understated softness ("so cute", "ahhhh").

### 3. Writing Modes
* **Private Chat:** Informal, multi-message bursts, lowercase.
* **Public / Sandwich Codes:** Polished grammar, but keeps the dry humour and practical explanations.
* **Product Explanation:** Focuses on trade-offs, constraints, and utility without buzzwords.

---

## Challenges

- **Calibrating Humour**: Keeping the humour dry and observational rather than forcing punchlines or acting like a caricature.
- **Balancing Informality**: Knowing when to use the lowercase, fragmented chat style vs. the clearer, structured public writing style.

---

## Future Improvements

- **Scenario Expansion**: Adding more specific templates for code reviews, architectural discussions, or responding to annoying emails.
